description: Canonical ReadyMCAT MCQ bank: merged bio/biochem, chem/phys and psych/soc section banks. Built into the pre-loaded 'ReadyMCAT' deck by readymcat/tools/build_question_bank.py; each note is tagged '#ReadyMCAT::AAMC::<category>' for the points-at-stake queue.

items.stem: All 20 standard amino acids share a central (alpha) carbon covalently bonded to which set of groups?
options: An amino group, a carboxyl group, a hydrogen atom, and a variable R group
A hydroxyl group, a carboxyl group, and two R groups
Two carboxyl groups, a hydrogen atom, and an R group
An amino group, a phosphate group, a hydrogen atom, and an R group
items.explanation: The alpha carbon of every standard amino acid is bonded to an amino group (-NH2/-NH3+), a carboxyl group (-COOH/-COO-), a hydrogen atom, and a distinguishing side chain (R). Standard amino acids have only one carboxyl and one amino group in the backbone (acidic side chains are separate), so 'two carboxyl groups' is wrong; a phosphate group is not part of the amino acid backbone; and any choice that omits the amino group or invents a second R group cannot describe the shared core.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.3 (Biological Macromolecules: Proteins)

subquestions.stem: Which functional group gives an amino acid its acidic character?
subquestions.explanation: The carboxyl group can donate a proton, making it acidic; the amino group accepts a proton and is basic.

subquestions.stem: What structural feature distinguishes one standard amino acid from another?
subquestions.explanation: Only the R group differs among the 20 standard amino acids; the backbone atoms are identical in all of them.

items.stem: Which amino acid has a nonpolar, hydrophobic side chain most likely to be buried in the interior of a globular protein?
items.explanation: Leucine has a branched aliphatic hydrocarbon side chain that is nonpolar and hydrophobic, so it tends to be buried away from water. Aspartate is acidic (negatively charged at pH 7), lysine is basic (positively charged), and serine is polar (hydroxyl-bearing); all three are hydrophilic and favor the solvent-exposed surface.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.3 (Biological Macromolecules: Proteins)

subquestions.stem: Where do hydrophobic side chains tend to end up in a folded globular protein in aqueous solution?
subquestions.explanation: The hydrophobic effect drives nonpolar residues into the protein interior, minimizing their contact with water.

subquestions.stem: Which pair lists two charged (ionizable) side chains?
subquestions.explanation: Aspartate is negatively charged and lysine is positively charged at physiological pH; the other pairs are nonpolar.

items.stem: A neutral amino acid such as glycine (side-chain has no ionizable group) exists predominantly as a zwitterion at physiological pH. Its isoelectric point (pI) is best calculated as:
options: The pKa of the carboxyl group alone
The average of the pKa of the carboxyl group and the pKa of the amino group
Always exactly 7.0 regardless of the amino acid
The pKa of the amino group alone
items.explanation: For an amino acid with no ionizable side chain, the net charge is zero midway between its two relevant pKa values, so pI = (pKa1 + pKa2)/2, the average of the alpha-carboxyl (~2) and alpha-amino (~9-10) pKa values, giving a pI near 6. Using only the carboxyl pKa or only the amino pKa ignores the other ionizable group, and the pI is not a fixed 7.0 because it depends on the specific pKa values of each amino acid.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e (acid-base behavior of amino acids)

subquestions.stem: What is a zwitterion?
options: A molecule with both a positive and a negative charge but no net charge
A molecule with two positive charges
A molecule with no charged groups at all
subquestions.explanation: At physiological pH the amino group is protonated (+) and the carboxyl is deprotonated (-), giving separate charges that cancel to a net charge of zero.

subquestions.stem: At the isoelectric point, the net charge on the amino acid is:
subquestions.explanation: The pI is by definition the pH at which the molecule carries no net charge.

items.stem: At blood pH (~7.4), which amino acid side chain is most likely to carry a net negative charge?
items.explanation: Glutamate has a carboxylic acid side chain (pKa ~4.1); at pH 7.4 it is deprotonated and negatively charged (-COO-). Arginine is basic and positively charged at pH 7.4. Valine and phenylalanine have nonpolar side chains that carry no charge.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.3 (Biological Macromolecules: Proteins)

subquestions.stem: A side chain is negatively charged when its pH is ____ its pKa.
subquestions.explanation: When pH is above the pKa of an acidic group, it is mostly deprotonated and therefore negatively charged.

subquestions.stem: Which two amino acids have acidic (carboxylate) side chains?
subquestions.explanation: Aspartate and glutamate carry carboxyl side chains that are negatively charged near physiological pH.

items.stem: Two amino acids are joined into a dipeptide. The bond that forms and the byproduct released are:
options: An amide (peptide) bond, releasing a molecule of water
A phosphodiester bond, releasing a molecule of water
A glycosidic bond, releasing carbon dioxide
items.explanation: A peptide bond is an amide linkage formed between the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the amino group of the next in a dehydration (condensation) reaction that releases one water molecule. Phosphodiester bonds join nucleotides, glycosidic bonds join sugars, and disulfide bonds link two cysteine side chains; none is the linkage between amino acid backbones.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.3 (Biological Macromolecules: Proteins)

subquestions.stem: A reaction that joins two monomers and releases water is called a:
subquestions.explanation: Dehydration synthesis links monomers while removing water; hydrolysis is the reverse, using water to break the bond.

subquestions.stem: A peptide bond forms between the carboxyl group of one amino acid and which group of the next?
subquestions.explanation: The carboxyl carbon bonds to the nitrogen of the incoming amino acid's amino group, forming the amide (peptide) bond.

items.stem: The peptide bond shows restricted rotation and is essentially planar. The best explanation is that:
options: Resonance gives the C-N bond partial double-bond character
Hydrogen bonding freezes the bond in place
The bond is a true carbon-carbon double bond
items.explanation: Delocalization of the carbonyl electrons gives the peptide (C-N) bond partial double-bond character, which prevents free rotation and holds the six atoms of the peptide group in a plane (usually the trans configuration). The peptide bond is not a true carbon-carbon double bond, and although hydrogen bonds and disulfide bridges stabilize higher-order structure, neither is the cause of peptide-bond planarity.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry: Fundamentals of Biochemistry (Jakubowski & Flatt), Protein Structure

subquestions.stem: Partial double-bond character in a bond generally causes:
subquestions.explanation: Double-bond (or partial double-bond) character resists rotation, locking the connected atoms into a fixed geometry.

subquestions.stem: The planar unit created by resonance in the backbone helps define which level of structure through phi/psi angles?
subquestions.explanation: Because the peptide unit is planar, backbone conformation is set by rotation about the alpha carbon (phi/psi angles), which determines secondary structure such as helices and sheets.

items.stem: The alpha helix and beta pleated sheet are elements of secondary structure. Both are stabilized primarily by:
options: Ionic bonds between charged R groups
Peptide bonds between adjacent side chains
Hydrogen bonds between backbone carbonyl oxygen and amide hydrogen atoms
items.explanation: Secondary structure arises from hydrogen bonding between backbone amide (N-H) hydrogens and carbonyl (C=O) oxygens; the pattern of these bonds distinguishes helices from sheets. Peptide bonds define primary structure. Disulfide and ionic bonds stabilize tertiary structure through side-chain interactions, not the backbone hydrogen-bond network of secondary structure.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.3 (Biological Macromolecules: Proteins)

subquestions.stem: Primary structure refers to:
options: The linear sequence of amino acids
subquestions.explanation: Primary structure is the covalently bonded order of amino acids in the polypeptide chain.

subquestions.stem: Secondary structure hydrogen bonds involve atoms of the:
subquestions.explanation: Backbone C=O and N-H groups hydrogen bond to form helices and sheets, independent of the side chains.

items.stem: Which interaction that stabilizes tertiary structure is a COVALENT bond?
options: A hydrophobic interaction between nonpolar side chains
A hydrogen bond between polar side chains
A salt bridge between oppositely charged side chains
A disulfide bond between two cysteine side chains
items.explanation: A disulfide bond is a covalent S-S linkage formed by oxidation of two cysteine thiol (-SH) groups. Hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonds, and salt bridges (ionic interactions) all help fold and stabilize tertiary structure but are noncovalent.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.3 (Biological Macromolecules: Proteins)

subquestions.stem: Which amino acid can form disulfide bonds?
subquestions.explanation: Cysteine's thiol (-SH) side chain is oxidized to form the covalent S-S disulfide bond.

subquestions.stem: Tertiary structure describes:
options: The overall three-dimensional shape of a single polypeptide
The pairing of two DNA strands
subquestions.explanation: Tertiary structure is the complete 3-D fold of one polypeptide, driven by side-chain interactions.

items.stem: Hemoglobin consists of four polypeptide subunits assembled into a functional unit. This arrangement is an example of which level of protein structure?
items.explanation: Quaternary structure is the assembly of two or more folded polypeptide subunits into a functional protein, as in hemoglobin's four chains. Primary is the sequence, secondary is local helices/sheets, and tertiary is the 3-D fold of a single chain; only quaternary describes multi-subunit assembly.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.3 (Biological Macromolecules: Proteins)

subquestions.stem: A protein made of a single polypeptide chain can have which of the following?
options: Primary, secondary, and tertiary structure but not quaternary
subquestions.explanation: Quaternary structure requires more than one polypeptide; a single-chain protein can reach only tertiary structure.

subquestions.stem: Quaternary structure is held together by:
options: The same noncovalent (and sometimes disulfide) interactions between subunits
subquestions.explanation: Subunits associate through hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds, and sometimes interchain disulfides, not new peptide bonds.

items.stem: When a globular protein is heated until it denatures and loses activity, which statement is generally TRUE?
options: The protein is converted into individual free amino acids
The peptide bonds of the primary structure are hydrolyzed
Noncovalent interactions and higher-order structure are disrupted, but the primary sequence (peptide bonds) remains intact
The amino acid sequence is scrambled into a new order
items.explanation: Denaturation disrupts the noncovalent interactions (and can break disulfide bonds) that maintain secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure, causing loss of function, but it does not break the peptide bonds that define primary structure. Therefore the sequence is unchanged (not scrambled) and the protein is not hydrolyzed into free amino acids.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.3 (Biological Macromolecules: Proteins)

subquestions.stem: Which level of structure is preserved during denaturation?
subquestions.explanation: Only the covalent peptide-bond sequence (primary structure) survives; the higher-order folds are lost.

subquestions.stem: A common consequence of denaturing an enzyme is:
options: A permanent increase in catalytic rate
subquestions.explanation: Because the active site depends on precise 3-D folding, denaturation typically abolishes activity.

items.stem: An enzyme speeds up a biochemical reaction. Compared with the uncatalyzed reaction, the enzyme:
options: Raises the activation energy so more product forms
Lowers the activation energy without changing the free-energy difference (ΔG) between reactants and products
Makes an endergonic reaction exergonic by changing ΔG
Is permanently consumed in the reaction
items.explanation: Enzymes accelerate reactions by lowering the activation energy (stabilizing the transition state); they do not change the overall ΔG, the equilibrium position, or whether a reaction is spontaneous. They are not consumed and can be reused. An enzyme cannot make a nonspontaneous reaction spontaneous on its own.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.6 (Metabolism: Enzymes)

subquestions.stem: Activation energy is best described as:
options: The difference in free energy between products and reactants
The energy barrier that must be overcome to reach the transition state
The total energy released by a reaction
subquestions.explanation: Activation energy (Ea) is the input required to reach the transition state; catalysts lower it.

subquestions.stem: Does an enzyme change the equilibrium constant (Keq) of a reaction?
options: Yes; it shifts equilibrium toward products
Yes; it shifts equilibrium toward reactants
No; it speeds the approach to the same equilibrium
subquestions.explanation: Enzymes speed both forward and reverse rates equally, so the equilibrium position (Keq) is unchanged.

items.stem: The 'induced fit' model of enzyme action states that:
options: The substrate must already match the active site perfectly like a rigid lock and key
Any substrate can bind any enzyme with equal affinity
The active site changes shape slightly upon substrate binding to improve catalysis
Enzymes covalently and permanently incorporate their substrates
items.explanation: In the induced fit model, substrate binding causes a conformational change in the active site that optimizes contacts and strains the substrate toward the transition state. The rigid 'lock and key' description is the older, less accurate picture. Enzymes are also highly specific (not equal affinity for all substrates), and they release products rather than permanently incorporating substrate.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.6 (Metabolism: Enzymes)

subquestions.stem: The specific region of an enzyme where substrate binds and catalysis occurs is the:
subquestions.explanation: Substrate binds and is converted in the active site; allosteric sites are separate regulatory sites.

subquestions.stem: Enzyme specificity means an enzyme typically:
options: Acts on one substrate or a narrow class of substrates
Catalyzes every reaction in the cell
subquestions.explanation: The complementary shape and chemistry of the active site make each enzyme selective for particular substrates.

items.stem: In Michaelis-Menten kinetics, the Michaelis constant (Km) is the substrate concentration at which the reaction proceeds at half of Vmax. A LOWER Km generally indicates:
options: That the enzyme is fully saturated at all substrate concentrations
Higher affinity of the enzyme for its substrate
Lower affinity of the enzyme for its substrate
items.explanation: Km equals the substrate concentration giving half-maximal velocity; a low Km means half-maximal rate is reached at low substrate, reflecting high substrate affinity. A high Km reflects low affinity. Km is distinct from Vmax (the maximum rate), and no enzyme is saturated at all concentrations.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry: Fundamentals of Biochemistry (Jakubowski & Flatt), Enzyme Kinetics

subquestions.stem: Vmax is reached when:
options: The inhibitor is at its highest
The enzyme is saturated with substrate
subquestions.explanation: At saturating substrate, essentially all active sites are occupied and the rate plateaus at Vmax.

subquestions.stem: At a substrate concentration equal to Km, the reaction velocity is:
subquestions.explanation: By definition, Km is the substrate concentration that gives half the maximal velocity.

items.stem: A competitive inhibitor binds reversibly to the active site of an enzyme. On a Michaelis-Menten plot, adding a competitive inhibitor:
options: Has no effect on either Km or Vmax
Increases the apparent Km while Vmax is unchanged
Decreases Vmax while Km is unchanged
items.explanation: Because a competitive inhibitor competes with substrate for the active site, adding more substrate overcomes it, so Vmax is still reachable but more substrate is needed for half-maximal rate, raising the apparent Km. A decreased Vmax with an unchanged Km instead describes noncompetitive inhibition, and competitive inhibition neither decreases both constants nor leaves them both unchanged.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry: Fundamentals of Biochemistry (Jakubowski & Flatt), Enzyme Inhibition

subquestions.stem: A competitive inhibitor binds where on the enzyme?
options: An allosteric site far from the active site
subquestions.explanation: Competitive inhibitors resemble substrate and occupy the active site, blocking substrate binding.

subquestions.stem: Competitive inhibition can be overcome by:
subquestions.explanation: Adding excess substrate outcompetes the inhibitor for the active site, restoring Vmax.

items.stem: A pure noncompetitive inhibitor binds equally well to the free enzyme and the enzyme-substrate complex at a site distinct from the active site. Its classic kinetic signature is:
options: No change in Km or Vmax
Decreased Vmax with an unchanged Km
Increased Km with an unchanged Vmax
Increased Vmax with a decreased Km
items.explanation: A pure noncompetitive inhibitor reduces the amount of functional enzyme regardless of substrate, so it lowers Vmax; because it binds a separate site and does not compete with substrate binding, the substrate's apparent affinity (Km) is unchanged. An increased Km with an unchanged Vmax would instead indicate competitive inhibition, and a pure noncompetitive inhibitor does not raise Vmax or leave it unchanged.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry: Fundamentals of Biochemistry (Jakubowski & Flatt), Enzyme Inhibition

subquestions.stem: A noncompetitive inhibitor binds:
options: The DNA that encodes the enzyme
An allosteric site separate from the active site
subquestions.explanation: Noncompetitive inhibitors bind a distinct (allosteric) site, altering the enzyme so catalysis is less effective.

subquestions.stem: Why can't extra substrate reverse pure noncompetitive inhibition?
options: Because the inhibitor does not compete for the active site
subquestions.explanation: Since the inhibitor binds elsewhere, adding substrate does not displace it, so Vmax stays reduced.

items.stem: In feedback (end-product) inhibition of a metabolic pathway, the final product typically:
options: Increases the activity of every enzyme in the pathway
Covalently destroys the first enzyme in the pathway
Binds an allosteric site on an early enzyme and decreases its activity
Acts only by lowering the temperature of the cell
items.explanation: Feedback inhibition regulates flux: the end product binds allosterically to an enzyme early in the pathway (often the committed/rate-limiting step) and reduces its activity, preventing overproduction. It does not permanently destroy the enzyme, does not activate the whole pathway, and does not work through temperature.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.6 (Metabolism: Enzyme regulation)

subquestions.stem: An allosteric site is:
options: The same as the active site
A regulatory site separate from the active site
subquestions.explanation: Allosteric regulators bind a site other than the active site and change the enzyme's shape/activity.

subquestions.stem: Feedback inhibition primarily prevents the cell from:
subquestions.explanation: By throttling an early step once product accumulates, the cell conserves resources and avoids excess product.

items.stem: Many enzymes require a nonprotein helper for activity. Which statement correctly distinguishes cofactors and coenzymes?
options: Coenzymes are segments of DNA that bind the enzyme
Coenzymes are small organic molecules (often vitamin-derived), whereas cofactors are commonly inorganic ions such as Zn2+ or Mg2+
Coenzymes are always metal ions, whereas cofactors are always proteins
Both terms refer only to the substrate of the reaction
items.explanation: Cofactors are inorganic helpers (metal ions like Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe2+); coenzymes are organic helper molecules, many derived from vitamins (e.g., NAD+ from niacin, FAD from riboflavin, coenzyme A from pantothenate). Coenzymes are not metal ions, cofactors are not proteins, and neither is the substrate or a DNA segment.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.6 (Metabolism: Enzymes, cofactors and coenzymes)

subquestions.stem: NAD+ and FAD are examples of:
subquestions.explanation: NAD+ (from niacin) and FAD (from riboflavin) are organic, vitamin-derived coenzymes that carry electrons.

subquestions.stem: A metal ion such as Mg2+ that an enzyme needs to function is best called a:
subquestions.explanation: Inorganic ion helpers are cofactors; organic helpers are coenzymes.

items.stem: In double-stranded DNA, the two strands are antiparallel and held together by hydrogen bonds between complementary bases. Which base pairing and bonding is correct?
options: All base pairs are held by a single hydrogen bond
Adenine pairs with cytosine via three hydrogen bonds; guanine pairs with thymine via two
Adenine pairs with guanine via two hydrogen bonds; thymine pairs with cytosine via three
Adenine pairs with thymine via two hydrogen bonds; guanine pairs with cytosine via three
items.explanation: In DNA, A pairs with T through two hydrogen bonds and G pairs with C through three hydrogen bonds (a purine always pairs with a pyrimidine). The other options mispair bases (purine-purine or wrong partners) or misstate the number of hydrogen bonds; the three G-C bonds make GC-rich DNA more thermally stable.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.14 (DNA Structure and Function)

subquestions.stem: 'Antiparallel' strands means that:
options: The strands never form hydrogen bonds
Both strands run 5' to 3' in the same direction
The strands are identical in sequence
One strand runs 5' to 3' while its partner runs 3' to 5'
subquestions.explanation: The two DNA strands point in opposite directions, which is required for complementary base pairing and replication.

subquestions.stem: A purine (A or G) always pairs with a:
subquestions.explanation: Purine-pyrimidine pairing keeps the helix a constant width; purine-purine or pyrimidine-pyrimidine pairs would not fit.

items.stem: DNA replication is described as 'semiconservative.' This means each daughter DNA molecule contains:
options: A random mixture of RNA and DNA in both strands
One original (parental) strand and one newly synthesized strand
items.explanation: In semiconservative replication each parental strand serves as a template, so every daughter duplex has one conserved parental strand and one new strand. Two new strands would be conservative/dispersive misconceptions, two parental strands would mean no new synthesis, and the strands are DNA, not an RNA mixture.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.14 (DNA Replication)

subquestions.stem: Each parental strand acts as a:
options: Template for a new complementary strand
subquestions.explanation: Base-pairing rules let each old strand template an exact new complementary strand.

subquestions.stem: Semiconservative replication helps ensure:
options: Faithful copying of the genetic sequence
Loss of half the genes each cycle
subquestions.explanation: Using each strand as a template preserves the original sequence in both daughter molecules.

items.stem: DNA polymerase synthesizes new DNA only in the 5' to 3' direction and cannot start a strand from scratch. Which enzyme provides the short starting segment it needs?
options: Helicase, which adds the first nucleotide
Primase, which lays down an RNA primer
items.explanation: DNA polymerase extends a free 3'-OH but cannot initiate de novo, so primase synthesizes a short RNA primer to provide that 3'-OH. Helicase unwinds the double helix, ligase seals nicks between fragments, and topoisomerase relieves supercoiling; none of these lays down the primer.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.14 (DNA Replication)

subquestions.stem: DNA polymerase adds nucleotides to which end of the growing strand?
subquestions.explanation: New nucleotides are added to the 3'-OH, so synthesis proceeds 5' to 3'.

subquestions.stem: The enzyme that unwinds and separates the parental DNA strands is:
subquestions.explanation: Helicase breaks the hydrogen bonds between strands to open the replication fork.

items.stem: Because DNA polymerase works only 5' to 3', the lagging strand is synthesized:
options: In the 3' to 5' direction to keep up with the fork
Discontinuously as Okazaki fragments that are later joined by DNA ligase
Only after the entire leading strand is degraded
Continuously in one piece toward the replication fork
items.explanation: The lagging strand runs opposite to fork movement, so it is made in short 5'-to-3' pieces (Okazaki fragments) that are subsequently connected by DNA ligase. The leading strand, not the lagging strand, is continuous; no DNA polymerase synthesizes 3' to 5'; and the leading strand is not degraded.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.14 (DNA Replication)

subquestions.stem: Okazaki fragments are found on the:
subquestions.explanation: The discontinuous lagging strand is built from many short Okazaki fragments.

subquestions.stem: Which enzyme joins adjacent Okazaki fragments into a continuous strand?
subquestions.explanation: DNA ligase forms the phosphodiester bond that seals the nick between fragments.

items.stem: During transcription in a cell, RNA polymerase synthesizes an mRNA molecule. It reads the DNA template strand in the 3' to 5' direction and builds the RNA:
options: 3' to 5', using thymine as in DNA
5' to 3', but only after a DNA primer is added
5' to 3', using uracil in place of thymine
items.explanation: RNA polymerase reads the template (3'->5') and synthesizes RNA 5'->3', incorporating uracil instead of thymine. Unlike DNA polymerase, RNA polymerase does not require a primer, and synthesis proceeds in a single direction along the template.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.15 (Genes and Proteins: Transcription)

subquestions.stem: Which base replaces thymine in RNA?
subquestions.explanation: RNA uses uracil (U) where DNA uses thymine (T); U pairs with A.

subquestions.stem: The DNA region where RNA polymerase binds to begin transcription is the:
subquestions.explanation: The promoter (containing signals such as the TATA box) positions RNA polymerase at the start of a gene.

items.stem: Which set of modifications is applied to a eukaryotic pre-mRNA before it leaves the nucleus?
options: Addition of a DNA primer at the 3' end
Addition of a 5' cap, addition of a 3' poly-A tail, and removal of introns by splicing
Translation into protein while still in the nucleus
Removal of exons and retention of introns
items.explanation: Eukaryotic pre-mRNA receives a 5' 7-methylguanosine cap and a 3' poly-A tail, and the spliceosome removes introns while joining exons. Introns (not exons) are removed, no DNA primer is added to mRNA, and translation occurs in the cytoplasm, not the nucleus.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.15 (Genes and Proteins: RNA processing)

subquestions.stem: Introns are:
options: The coding sequences kept in mature mRNA
subquestions.explanation: Introns are intervening noncoding segments spliced out; exons are the expressed coding segments retained.

subquestions.stem: The 5' cap and poly-A tail help to:
options: Protect mRNA and aid its export and translation
subquestions.explanation: These modifications stabilize the transcript and promote nuclear export and ribosome binding.

items.stem: The genetic code is described as 'degenerate' (redundant). This means that:
options: Most amino acids are specified by more than one codon
The code differs completely between all species
Each amino acid has exactly one codon
One codon can code for several different amino acids depending on context
items.explanation: Degeneracy means multiple codons can specify the same amino acid (e.g., six codons for leucine), which buffers some mutations. The code is also unambiguous (one codon specifies only one amino acid, so a single codon cannot code for several different amino acids), nearly universal across species rather than completely different in every species, and redundant rather than strictly one codon per amino acid.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.15 (Genes and Proteins: The Genetic Code)

subquestions.stem: How many nucleotides make up one codon?
subquestions.explanation: Codons are read as triplets; 3 nucleotides specify one amino acid or a stop signal.

subquestions.stem: The codon AUG serves as the start codon and codes for:
subquestions.explanation: AUG initiates translation and encodes methionine.

items.stem: During elongation in translation, a peptide bond is formed between the growing chain and the next amino acid. This reaction is catalyzed by:
options: tRNA acting alone without the ribosome
The ribosome's rRNA (peptidyl transferase activity, a ribozyme)
items.explanation: Peptide bond formation is catalyzed by the large ribosomal subunit's rRNA, which acts as a ribozyme (peptidyl transferase). DNA polymerase has no role in translation, the 5' cap aids ribosome loading but is not catalytic, and tRNA delivers amino acids but does not itself catalyze peptide bond formation.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.15 (Genes and Proteins: Translation)

subquestions.stem: The tRNA anticodon carries out its job by base pairing with the:
options: Ribosomal small subunit rRNA start site
subquestions.explanation: Codon-anticodon pairing ensures the correct amino acid is delivered for each codon.

subquestions.stem: In which ribosomal site does the incoming aminoacyl-tRNA first bind?
subquestions.explanation: Charged tRNAs enter at the A site; the P site holds the peptidyl-tRNA and the E site releases spent tRNA.

items.stem: Which RNA molecule delivers amino acids to the ribosome and pairs its anticodon with an mRNA codon?
items.explanation: tRNA carries a specific amino acid and uses its anticodon to read the mRNA codon. mRNA carries the coding message, rRNA is a structural and catalytic component of the ribosome, and snRNA participates in splicing; none of these delivers amino acids.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.15 (Genes and Proteins)

subquestions.stem: Which RNA carries the genetic message from DNA to the ribosome?
subquestions.explanation: Messenger RNA transports the transcribed code to the ribosome for translation.

subquestions.stem: Ribosomal RNA (rRNA) primarily functions to:
options: Form the structural and catalytic core of the ribosome
subquestions.explanation: rRNA makes up much of the ribosome and catalyzes peptide bond formation.

items.stem: A single base substitution changes a codon from UGG (tryptophan) to UGA. This is best classified as a:
options: Frameshift mutation, because the reading frame shifts
Nonsense mutation, because a premature stop codon is created
Silent mutation, because the amino acid is unchanged
Missense mutation, because a different amino acid is inserted
items.explanation: UGA is a stop codon, so changing a coding codon into a stop is a nonsense mutation that truncates the protein. A silent mutation would keep the same amino acid, a frameshift results from insertions/deletions that are not multiples of three, and a missense mutation swaps one amino acid for another rather than creating a stop.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.14 (Mutations)

subquestions.stem: A mutation that changes one codon so it encodes a different amino acid is called:
subquestions.explanation: Missense mutations substitute one amino acid for another.

subquestions.stem: Insertion or deletion of one nucleotide most often causes a:
options: Change limited to a single codon
subquestions.explanation: Adding or removing one base shifts the triplet reading frame for everything downstream.

items.stem: The lac operon of E. coli is an INDUCIBLE operon. In the absence of lactose, transcription of the lac genes is:
options: Turned on because the repressor binds the operator
Turned off because the lac repressor binds the operator
Turned on because lactose is the corepressor
items.explanation: Without lactose, the active lac repressor binds the operator and blocks RNA polymerase, so the genes are off. When lactose is present, its isomer allolactose (the inducer) binds and inactivates the repressor, allowing transcription. Lactose is an inducer, not a corepressor, and the operon is not constitutively on.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.16 (Gene Expression: Prokaryotic regulation)

subquestions.stem: An inducible operon is normally:
options: Off, and switched on by a specific signal
On, and switched off by a specific signal
subquestions.explanation: Inducible operons (often catabolic) stay off until an inducer turns them on.

subquestions.stem: The inducer that inactivates the lac repressor is derived from:
subquestions.explanation: Allolactose, made from lactose, binds the repressor and relieves repression.

items.stem: The trp operon is a REPRESSIBLE operon that produces enzymes for tryptophan synthesis. When tryptophan levels are high, tryptophan acts as a corepressor and:
options: Activates the repressor so it binds the operator and shuts the operon off
Binds RNA polymerase to speed transcription
Inactivates the repressor so transcription increases
items.explanation: In a repressible operon, abundant end product (tryptophan) binds the inactive repressor as a corepressor, activating it to bind the operator and stop transcription, so the cell stops making trp when it already has enough. It does not increase transcription, delete genes, or accelerate RNA polymerase.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.16 (Gene Expression: Prokaryotic regulation)

subquestions.stem: A repressible operon is normally:
options: On, and switched off when its end product accumulates
Off, and switched on by an inducer
subquestions.explanation: Repressible operons (often anabolic) run until enough product accumulates to shut them off.

subquestions.stem: A corepressor works by:
options: Binding and activating an otherwise inactive repressor
Binding and inactivating an active repressor
subquestions.explanation: The corepressor converts the repressor to its DNA-binding (active) form.

items.stem: In eukaryotes, distant DNA sequences called enhancers increase transcription of a gene by:
options: Binding activator proteins and looping the DNA to contact the promoter
Encoding the amino acid sequence of the protein
Serving as the ribosome binding site on mRNA
items.explanation: Enhancers bind specific activator transcription factors, and DNA looping brings these bound activators into contact with the promoter/general machinery to boost transcription, even from far away. Enhancers are DNA regulatory elements, not ribosome binding sites, do not encode protein, and do not remove the promoter.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.16 (Gene Expression: Eukaryotic regulation)

subquestions.stem: Proteins that bind DNA regulatory sequences to control transcription are called:
subquestions.explanation: Transcription factors (activators or repressors) bind promoters/enhancers to regulate gene expression.

subquestions.stem: Enhancers can act even when located far from the gene because:
options: DNA can loop to bring them near the promoter
They diffuse through the nuclear membrane
subquestions.explanation: Looping of the chromosome positions distant enhancer-bound activators next to the promoter.

items.stem: Addition of methyl groups to cytosine bases in a gene's promoter region (DNA methylation) is generally associated with:
options: A change in the underlying DNA sequence
items.explanation: DNA methylation of promoter cytosines typically compacts chromatin and represses transcription without altering the DNA sequence, an epigenetic change. It does not increase transcription of the methylated gene, does not mutate the sequence, and is unrelated to direct translation. (Histone acetylation, by contrast, tends to open chromatin and increase transcription.)

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.16 (Gene Expression: Epigenetic regulation)

subquestions.stem: Epigenetic modifications change gene expression:
options: Without altering the DNA base sequence
subquestions.explanation: Epigenetic marks (methylation, histone modification) affect expression while leaving the sequence intact.

subquestions.stem: Histone acetylation generally:
subquestions.explanation: Acetylation neutralizes histone charge, loosening DNA-histone contacts and increasing accessibility.

items.stem: The polymerase chain reaction (PCR) amplifies a target DNA sequence. The step performed at ~95 degrees C accomplishes which task?
options: Extension: DNA polymerase synthesizing new strands
Annealing: allowing primers to bind the template
Denaturation: separating the double-stranded DNA into single strands
Ligation: joining fragments into a plasmid
items.explanation: The high-temperature (~95 C) step denatures DNA by breaking the hydrogen bonds between strands. Annealing occurs at a lower temperature (~50-65 C) to let primers bind, and extension occurs near 72 C where a heat-stable polymerase (e.g., Taq) synthesizes new strands; ligation is not a PCR step.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.17 (Biotechnology and Genomics)

subquestions.stem: Why is a heat-stable polymerase (such as Taq) used in PCR?
options: It works only at room temperature
It synthesizes RNA instead of DNA
It survives the repeated high-temperature denaturation steps
subquestions.explanation: Taq polymerase remains active through repeated 95 C cycles that would denature ordinary polymerases.

subquestions.stem: Each complete PCR cycle ideally causes the amount of target DNA to:
subquestions.explanation: Because both strands are copied each cycle, the target amount grows exponentially (roughly 2^n).

items.stem: During agarose gel electrophoresis of DNA fragments, the fragments move through the gel toward the positive electrode. This occurs because DNA:
options: Binds the gel and cannot migrate
Is positively charged due to its nitrogenous bases
Is negatively charged due to its phosphate backbone
Is neutral and moves only by diffusion
items.explanation: The sugar-phosphate backbone gives DNA a uniform negative charge, so in an electric field it migrates toward the positive electrode (anode); smaller fragments travel faster/farther through the gel matrix. DNA is not positively charged or neutral, and it does migrate through the porous gel.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.17 (Biotechnology and Genomics)

subquestions.stem: In a gel, smaller DNA fragments:
options: Migrate less far than larger fragments
subquestions.explanation: Smaller fragments slip through the gel pores more easily and therefore travel farther in a given time.

subquestions.stem: The negative charge of DNA comes from its:
subquestions.explanation: Each phosphate in the backbone carries a negative charge at neutral pH.

items.stem: Restriction endonucleases are useful in cloning because they:
options: Synthesize DNA from an RNA template
Randomly cut DNA at any position
Cut DNA at specific recognition sequences, often leaving complementary 'sticky' ends
Add a poly-A tail to DNA
items.explanation: Restriction enzymes recognize specific (often palindromic) sequences and cut there; staggered cuts create single-stranded 'sticky' ends that base-pair with complementary ends of other fragments, allowing DNA ligase to join them into recombinant molecules. They are sequence-specific (not random), do not synthesize DNA (that is reverse transcriptase/polymerase), and do not add poly-A tails.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.17 (Biotechnology and Genomics)

subquestions.stem: Sticky ends are useful because they:
options: Base-pair with complementary ends of other DNA fragments
subquestions.explanation: Complementary single-stranded overhangs anneal, letting ligase join fragments from different sources.

subquestions.stem: After two fragments anneal at their sticky ends, which enzyme seals them together?
subquestions.explanation: DNA ligase forms the phosphodiester bonds that covalently join the annealed fragments.

items.stem: A researcher wants to detect a specific PROTEIN in a sample. Which technique is appropriate?
items.explanation: A Western blot separates proteins by electrophoresis and detects a target protein with specific antibodies. A Southern blot detects DNA, a Northern blot detects RNA, and PCR amplifies DNA; none of these detects protein directly.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.17 (Biotechnology and Genomics)

subquestions.stem: A Southern blot is used to detect:
subquestions.explanation: Southern = DNA; a common mnemonic is 'SNoW DRoP' (Southern-DNA, Northern-RNA, Western-Protein).

subquestions.stem: Western blots identify their target protein using:
subquestions.explanation: Antibodies bind the specific protein of interest for detection.

items.stem: Mendel's law of segregation states that:
options: Dominant alleles are always more common in a population
The two alleles for a gene separate during gamete formation, so each gamete carries only one allele
Both alleles are passed together into every gamete
Alleles of different genes always stay together during inheritance
items.explanation: The law of segregation says the paired alleles of a gene separate (segregate) during meiosis so each gamete receives just one allele. Claims that alleles stay together, or that both alleles pass into every gamete, contradict segregation, and how common a dominant allele is in a population is unrelated to the law of segregation.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.12 (Mendel's Experiments and Heredity)

subquestions.stem: An organism's two alleles for a gene are separated into different gametes during:
subquestions.explanation: Meiosis produces haploid gametes, each carrying one allele of each gene.

subquestions.stem: A heterozygous individual (Aa) produces gametes in what allele ratio?
subquestions.explanation: Segregation gives equal proportions of gametes carrying A and gametes carrying a.

items.stem: Mendel's law of independent assortment applies most cleanly to genes that are:
options: On different chromosomes (or far apart on the same chromosome)
Very close together on the same chromosome
Always inherited as a single unit
items.explanation: Independent assortment holds for genes on separate chromosomes, which line up randomly at metaphase I. Genes that are close together on the same chromosome are linked and tend to be inherited together, violating independent assortment. 'On the same allele' is nonsensical, and genes are not always inherited as one unit.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.12 (Mendel's Experiments and Heredity)

subquestions.stem: Independent assortment physically occurs when homologous chromosome pairs line up randomly during:
subquestions.explanation: Random orientation of each homolog pair at metaphase I mixes maternal and paternal chromosomes into gametes.

subquestions.stem: Genes located very close together on the same chromosome are said to be:
subquestions.explanation: Linked genes are inherited together more often than independent assortment predicts.

items.stem: Two organisms heterozygous for a completely dominant trait (Aa x Aa) are crossed. What phenotypic ratio is expected in the offspring?
options: All offspring show the recessive phenotype
items.explanation: An Aa x Aa cross yields genotypes 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa, so 3/4 show the dominant phenotype and 1/4 show the recessive phenotype (3:1). A 1:1 ratio is a test cross of Aa x aa, 9:3:3:1 is a dihybrid cross, and all-recessive would require both parents to be aa.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.12 (Mendel's Experiments and Heredity)

subquestions.stem: The genotypic ratio of an Aa x Aa cross is:
subquestions.explanation: A Punnett square of Aa x Aa gives 1:2:1 genotypes, which collapse to 3:1 phenotypes under full dominance.

subquestions.stem: What fraction of the offspring are homozygous recessive (aa)?
subquestions.explanation: Only the aa box (1 of 4) is homozygous recessive.

items.stem: Two individuals heterozygous for two independently assorting genes (AaBb x AaBb) are crossed. The expected phenotypic ratio of the offspring is:
items.explanation: For two independently assorting genes with complete dominance, AaBb x AaBb gives a 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio (9 both dominant : 3 dominant/recessive : 3 recessive/dominant : 1 both recessive). A 3:1 ratio is a monohybrid cross, 1:1:1:1 is a dihybrid test cross, and 9:7 reflects epistasis, not standard independent assortment.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.12 (Mendel's Experiments and Heredity)

subquestions.stem: The 9:3:3:1 ratio arises from combining two independent ____ ratios.
subquestions.explanation: Multiplying the two monohybrid 3:1 ratios (3:1)(3:1) yields 9:3:3:1.

subquestions.stem: What fraction of AaBb x AaBb offspring are recessive for both traits (aabb)?
subquestions.explanation: The probability aa (1/4) times bb (1/4) equals 1/16.

items.stem: To determine whether an organism showing the dominant phenotype is homozygous (AA) or heterozygous (Aa), the best cross is with an individual that is:
items.explanation: A test cross uses a homozygous recessive (aa) partner: if any recessive offspring appear, the unknown parent must be Aa; if all offspring show the dominant phenotype, it is likely AA. Crossing to AA masks the recessive allele, and crossing to an unknown or Aa parent does not cleanly reveal the genotype.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.12 (Mendel's Experiments and Heredity)

subquestions.stem: If a test cross (unknown x aa) produces some recessive offspring, the unknown parent is:
subquestions.explanation: Recessive offspring require an 'a' allele from the unknown parent, so it must be Aa.

subquestions.stem: A homozygous recessive individual is used because it:
options: Contributes only recessive alleles to offspring
subquestions.explanation: Its gametes all carry 'a', so offspring phenotypes directly reveal the unknown parent's alleles.

items.stem: In snapdragons, crossing a true-breeding red flower with a true-breeding white flower yields all pink offspring. This inheritance pattern is:
options: Incomplete dominance, in which the heterozygote shows an intermediate phenotype
Codominance, in which both alleles are fully and separately expressed
Complete dominance of red over white
items.explanation: A blended, intermediate phenotype (pink) in the heterozygote is the hallmark of incomplete dominance. In codominance both alleles would be expressed simultaneously and distinctly (e.g., red and white patches), complete dominance would give a red heterozygote, and nothing here indicates sex linkage.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.12 (Extensions of Mendelian genetics)

subquestions.stem: In incomplete dominance, the heterozygote's phenotype is:
options: A mix of both parental phenotypes shown separately
subquestions.explanation: Neither allele is fully dominant, so the heterozygote is a blend (e.g., pink).

subquestions.stem: Crossing two pink snapdragons (RR' x RR') gives what phenotypic ratio?
subquestions.explanation: With incomplete dominance, genotypes 1:2:1 map directly to three phenotypes 1 red : 2 pink : 1 white.

items.stem: The human ABO blood group illustrates both multiple alleles and codominance. An individual with genotype I^A I^B has which blood type?
options: Type A, because A is dominant to B
Type O, because the alleles cancel out
Type AB, because the A and B alleles are codominant
Type B, because B is dominant to A
items.explanation: I^A and I^B are codominant, so an I^A I^B genotype produces both A and B antigens and yields type AB blood. Neither A nor B is dominant over the other (ruling out type A or type B), and the alleles do not cancel to type O (which requires two recessive i alleles).

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.12 (Multiple alleles; blood types)

subquestions.stem: How many alleles exist in the ABO system, even though each person has only two?
subquestions.explanation: The ABO gene has three alleles in the population; any individual carries two of them.

subquestions.stem: Type O blood corresponds to which genotype?
subquestions.explanation: The i allele is recessive; only ii produces neither A nor B antigen (type O).

items.stem: For an X-linked recessive trait such as red-green color blindness, which pattern is expected?
options: Affected fathers pass the trait directly to all of their sons
The trait is more common in males, who need only one copy of the allele to be affected
The trait is more common in females, who need only one copy
The trait can never be passed to daughters
items.explanation: Because males are hemizygous (XY), a single recessive allele on their one X chromosome causes the trait, making X-linked recessive conditions more common in males. Females need two copies. An affected father passes his X to all daughters (making them carriers), not to sons (sons get his Y), so male-to-male transmission does not occur.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.13 (Modern Understanding of Inheritance: sex linkage)

subquestions.stem: Human males are described as 'hemizygous' for X-linked genes because they have:
subquestions.explanation: With a single X, males express whatever allele is on it, dominant or recessive.

subquestions.stem: An affected father (X-linked recessive) passes his X chromosome to:
subquestions.explanation: Daughters receive the father's X (becoming carriers or affected); sons receive his Y.

items.stem: Which event is unique to meiosis I (and does NOT occur in mitosis)?
options: Homologous chromosomes pair up and separate, halving the chromosome number
Sister chromatids separate to opposite poles
items.explanation: In meiosis I, homologous chromosomes pair (synapsis) and then separate, reducing the chromosome number from diploid to haploid (the reductional division). Separation of sister chromatids occurs in mitosis and in meiosis II, DNA replication precedes both mitosis and meiosis, and nuclear envelope breakdown is common to both.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.11 (Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction)

subquestions.stem: Meiosis produces gametes that are:
options: Genetically identical to the parent cell
subquestions.explanation: Two divisions with one round of replication yield four haploid cells.

subquestions.stem: Sister chromatids are separated during:
subquestions.explanation: Meiosis II resembles mitosis in separating sister chromatids; meiosis I separates homologs.

items.stem: Crossing over, a major source of genetic recombination, occurs when:
options: Homologous chromosomes exchange segments during prophase I of meiosis
Sister chromatids exchange identical segments during mitosis
Two different genes mutate at the same time
items.explanation: Crossing over is the reciprocal exchange of segments between non-sister chromatids of paired homologous chromosomes at chiasmata during prophase I, producing new allele combinations. Exchange between identical sister chromatids does not create new combinations, and neither simultaneous mutation nor fertilization is 'crossing over.'

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.11 (Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction)

subquestions.stem: Crossing over takes place during which phase of meiosis?
subquestions.explanation: Synapsis and chiasma formation occur in prophase I, enabling the exchange.

subquestions.stem: The main genetic consequence of crossing over is:
options: New combinations of alleles on a chromosome
subquestions.explanation: Recombining alleles increases genetic diversity among gametes without changing chromosome number.

items.stem: Nondisjunction during meiosis leads to gametes with an abnormal chromosome number. A resulting condition in which a cell has three copies of one chromosome is called:
items.explanation: Nondisjunction (failure of chromosomes or chromatids to separate) can give a gamete an extra chromosome; fertilization then produces a trisomic cell (e.g., trisomy 21). Polyploidy refers to whole extra chromosome sets, a translocation moves a segment between chromosomes, and a point mutation changes a single base, none of which describes a single extra chromosome.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.13 (Chromosomal basis of inheritance; nondisjunction)

subquestions.stem: Nondisjunction is the failure of chromosomes to:
subquestions.explanation: When homologs (meiosis I) or sister chromatids (meiosis II) fail to separate, daughter cells get the wrong number.

subquestions.stem: Aneuploidy refers to:
options: An extra complete set of chromosomes
An abnormal number of individual chromosomes
subquestions.explanation: Aneuploidy is a gain or loss of individual chromosomes (e.g., 2n+1 or 2n-1).

items.stem: In a testcross, two genes on the same chromosome show a recombination frequency of 50%. This result indicates the genes are:
options: Located at the exact same locus
Completely linked and always inherited together
So far apart (or on different chromosomes) that they behave as if unlinked
items.explanation: A recombination frequency of 50% is the maximum observed and is indistinguishable from independent assortment, so the genes behave as unlinked (very far apart or on different chromosomes). Completely linked genes show ~0% recombination, genes at the same locus are alleles of one gene, and 50% recombination shows crossing over is frequent, not absent.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.13 (Genetic linkage and map distance)

subquestions.stem: One map unit (centimorgan) corresponds to a recombination frequency of:
subquestions.explanation: Map distance in centimorgans equals percent recombinant offspring; 1 cM = 1% recombination.

subquestions.stem: Genes that are closer together on a chromosome show:
subquestions.explanation: Crossovers are less likely between nearby loci, so recombination frequency (and map distance) is small.

items.stem: In a population in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, a recessive genetic disorder affects 1 in 10,000 individuals (q^2 = 0.0001). Approximately what fraction of the population are heterozygous carriers?
items.explanation: If q^2 = 0.0001, then q = 0.01 and p = 0.99, so carrier frequency 2pq = 2(0.99)(0.01) is about 0.0198, roughly 2% or ~1 in 50. The value 0.01% (1 in 10,000) confuses carriers (2pq) with affected homozygotes (q^2), and 50% or 99% are far too high for such a rare allele.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.19 (The Evolution of Populations: Hardy-Weinberg)

subquestions.stem: In the Hardy-Weinberg equation p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1, the term 2pq represents:
subquestions.explanation: p^2 is dominant homozygotes, q^2 is recessive homozygotes, and 2pq is heterozygotes.

subquestions.stem: If q^2 = 0.0001, then q equals:
subquestions.explanation: q is the square root of q^2, so q = sqrt(0.0001) = 0.01.

items.stem: Which condition must be met for a population to remain in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (no change in allele frequencies)?
options: No natural selection, no mutation, no migration, random mating, and a very large population
A small population where drift is significant
items.explanation: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium requires no selection, no mutation, no migration (gene flow), random mating, and an infinitely large population (no genetic drift). Selection, small population size (drift), and gene flow all change allele frequencies and violate the assumptions.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.19 (The Evolution of Populations: Hardy-Weinberg)

subquestions.stem: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium describes a population that is:
options: Not evolving at the genetic level
subquestions.explanation: Equilibrium means allele and genotype frequencies stay constant across generations.

subquestions.stem: Which real-world factor most directly breaks Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium in a small population?
subquestions.explanation: Random fluctuations (drift) have a large effect when population size is small.

items.stem: In evolutionary biology, the 'fitness' of an organism is best defined as:
options: Its relative reproductive success (contribution of viable offspring to the next generation)
Its individual lifespan regardless of reproduction
The number of mutations it carries
items.explanation: Fitness measures relative reproductive success: how many surviving, reproducing offspring an individual contributes to the next generation. Strength/speed matter only insofar as they affect reproduction, lifespan alone does not equal fitness if the organism leaves no offspring, and mutation load is not the definition of fitness.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.18 (Evolution and the Origin of Species)

subquestions.stem: Natural selection acts directly on an organism's:
subquestions.explanation: Selection favors or disfavors phenotypes; allele frequencies change indirectly as a result.

subquestions.stem: For natural selection to cause evolution, the selected trait must be:
subquestions.explanation: Selection changes populations only when trait differences are heritable and vary among individuals.

items.stem: A small group of birds is blown by a storm to a remote island and founds a new population whose allele frequencies differ by chance from the mainland source. This is an example of:
options: The founder effect, a form of genetic drift
items.explanation: The founder effect occurs when a new population is started by a few individuals whose allele frequencies, by chance, are not representative of the original population, a special case of genetic drift. It is a random sampling effect, not selection, and it typically reduces (rather than increases) genetic diversity; gene flow would require ongoing movement between populations.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.19 (The Evolution of Populations: genetic drift)

subquestions.stem: Genetic drift has the largest effect in populations that are:
subquestions.explanation: Random sampling error dominates when only a few individuals contribute alleles.

subquestions.stem: If the island birds become geographically isolated and eventually can no longer interbreed with the mainland birds, this illustrates:
subquestions.explanation: Speciation driven by geographic separation is allopatric; reproductive isolation defines the new species.

items.stem: A reaction has a negative change in Gibbs free energy (ΔG < 0). This reaction is:
options: Exergonic and thermodynamically spontaneous (releases free energy)
At equilibrium with no net change
Endergonic and requires an input of energy
items.explanation: A negative ΔG means the reaction releases free energy and is spontaneous (exergonic) in the forward direction. Endergonic reactions have ΔG > 0, a reaction at equilibrium has ΔG = 0, and 'spontaneous' refers to thermodynamic favorability, not reaction speed, so it is certainly not impossible.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.6 (Metabolism: Energy and Metabolism)

subquestions.stem: 'Spontaneous' in thermodynamics means the reaction is:
options: Energetically favorable (will proceed without added energy)
subquestions.explanation: Spontaneity describes energetic favorability, not rate; a spontaneous reaction can still be slow.

subquestions.stem: In ΔG = ΔH - TΔS, a reaction is more likely to be spontaneous when:
options: ΔH is positive and ΔS is negative
ΔH is negative and ΔS is positive
subquestions.explanation: Releasing enthalpy (ΔH<0) and increasing entropy (ΔS>0) both drive ΔG negative.

items.stem: Cells use the hydrolysis of ATP to drive unfavorable reactions. This strategy is called energy coupling because:
options: ATP is converted permanently into DNA
The exergonic hydrolysis of ATP is linked to an endergonic reaction so the sum is favorable
ATP directly lowers activation energy like an enzyme
ATP hydrolysis absorbs energy from the surroundings
items.explanation: Energy coupling links the strongly exergonic hydrolysis of ATP (to ADP + Pi) with an endergonic process so their combined ΔG is negative and the overall reaction proceeds. ATP hydrolysis releases (not absorbs) energy, ATP is not a catalyst (that is the enzyme's role), and ATP is not turned into DNA.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.6 (Metabolism: ATP)

subquestions.stem: ATP hydrolysis releases energy primarily when it is converted to:
subquestions.explanation: Breaking the terminal phosphoanhydride bond yields ADP + Pi and releases free energy.

subquestions.stem: ATP is often called the cell's:
subquestions.explanation: ATP transfers usable chemical energy between reactions throughout the cell.

items.stem: When NAD+ is converted to NADH during catabolism, NAD+ has been:
options: Reduced, because it gained electrons (as a hydride)
items.explanation: Gaining electrons is reduction, so NAD+ accepting a hydride (two electrons and a proton) to become NADH is reduction; the fuel molecule that donated the electrons is oxidized. NAD+ is not phosphorylated (that would be NADP+ formation) or hydrolyzed in this step. Remember 'OIL RIG': Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain of electrons.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.7 (Cellular Respiration: redox reactions)

subquestions.stem: NADH and FADH2 function in metabolism as:
subquestions.explanation: They shuttle high-energy electrons to the electron transport chain.

subquestions.stem: A molecule that is oxidized has:
subquestions.explanation: Oxidation is loss of electrons; the electron acceptor is simultaneously reduced.

items.stem: For each glucose molecule that passes through glycolysis, the NET yield is:
items.explanation: Glycolysis splits one glucose into two pyruvate, investing 2 ATP and producing 4 ATP (net 2 ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation) plus 2 NADH. No CO2 is released in glycolysis itself (that occurs later), and FADH2 is generated in the citric acid cycle, not glycolysis.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.7 (Cellular Respiration: Glycolysis)

subquestions.stem: Glycolysis produces ATP by:
subquestions.explanation: Enzymes transfer a phosphate directly from an intermediate to ADP (substrate-level phosphorylation).

subquestions.stem: The end product of glycolysis that enters the mitochondrion (in aerobic conditions) is:
subquestions.explanation: Pyruvate is transported into the mitochondrion, where it is converted to acetyl-CoA.

items.stem: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm, and its main rate-limiting (committed) step is catalyzed by phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1). PFK-1 activity is:
options: Inhibited by high ATP and stimulated by AMP, matching flux to energy need
Independent of the cell's energy charge
Stimulated by high ATP and inhibited by AMP
items.explanation: PFK-1 is allosterically inhibited when ATP (and citrate) are abundant and activated by AMP, so glycolysis slows when energy is plentiful and speeds up when energy is low. It is the key regulated step of glycolysis, which occurs in the cytoplasm (not the mitochondrial matrix), and it is very much sensitive to the energy charge.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.7 (Cellular Respiration: regulation)

subquestions.stem: Glycolysis takes place in the:
subquestions.explanation: All ten glycolytic enzymes are soluble cytosolic enzymes.

subquestions.stem: A high ATP level in the cell signals that glycolysis should:
options: Stop making pyruvate entirely and reverse
subquestions.explanation: Abundant ATP inhibits PFK-1, throttling glucose breakdown when energy is plentiful.

items.stem: In the absence of oxygen, many cells carry out fermentation. The primary metabolic purpose of fermentation is to:
options: Produce large amounts of ATP directly from lactate
Regenerate NAD+ so that glycolysis can continue producing ATP
Generate oxygen for the electron transport chain
items.explanation: Fermentation oxidizes NADH back to NAD+ (e.g., by reducing pyruvate to lactate or to ethanol + CO2), replenishing the NAD+ that glycolysis needs so ATP production can continue without oxygen. Fermentation itself yields no additional ATP, does not generate O2, and does not make glucose (that is gluconeogenesis).

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.7 (Cellular Respiration: Fermentation)

subquestions.stem: In human muscle under anaerobic conditions, pyruvate is converted to:
subquestions.explanation: Lactate fermentation regenerates NAD+ in animals; yeast instead make ethanol and CO2.

subquestions.stem: Without regenerating NAD+, glycolysis would stop because:
options: NAD+ is needed as an electron acceptor in glycolysis
subquestions.explanation: Glycolysis requires NAD+ to oxidize glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate; without it, the pathway halts.

items.stem: Before the citric acid cycle, pyruvate is converted to acetyl-CoA by the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex. This reaction:
options: Consumes CO2 and produces ATP directly
Occurs in the cytoplasm and produces FADH2
Releases one CO2 and produces one NADH per pyruvate, in the mitochondrial matrix
items.explanation: The pyruvate dehydrogenase complex oxidatively decarboxylates pyruvate: it removes a carbon as CO2, reduces NAD+ to NADH, and attaches the remaining two-carbon acetyl group to coenzyme A, all in the mitochondrial matrix. It releases (not consumes) CO2, makes NADH (not FADH2 or direct ATP), and does not synthesize glucose.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.7 (Cellular Respiration: pyruvate oxidation)

subquestions.stem: The carbon removed from pyruvate during its conversion to acetyl-CoA leaves as:
subquestions.explanation: Oxidative decarboxylation releases one carbon as carbon dioxide.

subquestions.stem: Acetyl-CoA carries how many carbons into the citric acid cycle?
subquestions.explanation: The acetyl group is a two-carbon unit that condenses with oxaloacetate to form citrate.

items.stem: Per acetyl-CoA (one turn of the citric acid cycle), the electron carriers and high-energy products generated are:
options: 3 NADH, 1 FADH2, 1 GTP (or ATP), and 2 CO2
items.explanation: Each turn of the citric acid cycle (per acetyl-CoA) produces 3 NADH, 1 FADH2, 1 GTP/ATP (substrate-level), and releases 2 CO2. Because each glucose yields two acetyl-CoA, these numbers double per glucose. The other options misstate the per-turn stoichiometry or list glycolysis products.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.7 (Cellular Respiration: Citric Acid Cycle)

subquestions.stem: The citric acid cycle takes place in the:
subquestions.explanation: Its enzymes are located in the matrix (succinate dehydrogenase is embedded in the inner membrane).

subquestions.stem: Acetyl-CoA enters the cycle by combining with oxaloacetate to form:
subquestions.explanation: The two-carbon acetyl group plus four-carbon oxaloacetate makes the six-carbon citrate.

items.stem: In the electron transport chain (ETC) on the inner mitochondrial membrane, the FINAL electron acceptor is:
options: NAD+, which is reduced to NADH
Carbon dioxide, which is reduced to glucose
Molecular oxygen (O2), which is reduced to water
Pyruvate, which is reduced to lactate
items.explanation: Electrons flow through the ETC complexes and are ultimately passed to O2, the terminal electron acceptor, which combines with electrons and protons to form water. CO2 is not reduced to glucose here, NAD+ delivers electrons to the chain rather than accepting them at the end, and pyruvate reduction to lactate is fermentation.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.7 (Cellular Respiration: Oxidative Phosphorylation)

subquestions.stem: Why is oxygen essential for aerobic respiration?
options: It accepts electrons at the end of the ETC, keeping electrons flowing
subquestions.explanation: Without a terminal acceptor, the chain backs up and NADH/FADH2 cannot be reoxidized.

subquestions.stem: The high-energy electrons that enter the ETC are delivered mainly by:
subquestions.explanation: NADH and FADH2 carry electrons from glycolysis and the citric acid cycle to the chain.

items.stem: According to the chemiosmotic theory, most ATP in aerobic respiration is produced when:
options: Protons flow down their electrochemical gradient through ATP synthase
Glucose is split in the cytoplasm
Electrons are passed directly to ADP
items.explanation: The ETC pumps protons into the intermembrane space, creating a proton-motive force; protons then flow back through ATP synthase, and this flow drives the phosphorylation of ADP to ATP (chemiosmosis). ATP synthase does not oxidize NADH, electrons are not handed directly to ADP, and cytoplasmic glucose splitting is glycolysis, which yields little ATP.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.7 (Cellular Respiration: Oxidative Phosphorylation)

subquestions.stem: The proton gradient used by ATP synthase is established across the:
subquestions.explanation: Complexes I, III, and IV pump protons from the matrix into the intermembrane space across the inner membrane.

subquestions.stem: ATP synthase can be described as a molecular:
subquestions.explanation: Proton flow rotates part of ATP synthase, mechanically catalyzing ATP formation.

items.stem: Compared with complete aerobic respiration of glucose (~30-32 ATP), anaerobic glycolysis followed by fermentation yields only:
items.explanation: Without oxygen, cells rely on glycolysis (net 2 ATP) with fermentation only to regenerate NAD+, so the anaerobic yield is about 2 ATP per glucose. Aerobic respiration captures far more energy (~30-32 ATP) because the electron carriers feed oxidative phosphorylation; anaerobic metabolism is much less efficient, not more.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.7 (Cellular Respiration: ATP yield)

subquestions.stem: The large ATP advantage of aerobic respiration comes mainly from:
subquestions.explanation: Reoxidizing NADH/FADH2 through the ETC powers most of the ATP made per glucose.

subquestions.stem: Fermentation adds how much ATP beyond glycolysis?
subquestions.explanation: Fermentation's role is regenerating NAD+, not producing additional ATP.

items.stem: A chemical uncoupler makes the inner mitochondrial membrane leaky to protons. The expected effect is that:
options: Glycolysis is completely blocked in the cytoplasm
The proton gradient collapses, so ATP synthesis falls while energy is released as heat and electron transport speeds up
The electron transport chain stops entirely and no oxygen is consumed
items.explanation: If protons bypass ATP synthase through a leak, the electrochemical gradient dissipates, so ATP production drops even though electron transport (and O2 consumption) actually increases as the chain runs faster; the energy is lost as heat (the basis of nonshivering thermogenesis via UCP1). ATP synthesis does not rise, the ETC keeps running (often faster), and glycolysis is not directly blocked.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.7 (Cellular Respiration: Oxidative Phosphorylation)

subquestions.stem: ATP synthase normally makes ATP only because protons:
options: Freely diffuse across the membrane everywhere
Are prevented from crossing the membrane except through it
Are pumped by ATP synthase itself
subquestions.explanation: The membrane's impermeability forces protons through ATP synthase, coupling the gradient to ATP synthesis.

subquestions.stem: Brown adipose tissue generates heat using the uncoupling protein:
subquestions.explanation: UCP1 provides a regulated proton leak that dissipates the gradient as heat.

items.stem: Gluconeogenesis is the pathway that:
options: Oxidizes fatty acids in the mitochondria
Synthesizes glucose from noncarbohydrate precursors such as lactate, glycerol, and amino acids, mainly in the liver
Breaks glucose down into pyruvate to release ATP
items.explanation: Gluconeogenesis makes new glucose from noncarbohydrate sources (lactate, glycerol, glucogenic amino acids), predominantly in the liver, and is important during fasting. Breaking glucose down is glycolysis, storing it is glycogenesis, and oxidizing fatty acids is beta-oxidation, none of which is gluconeogenesis.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry: Fundamentals of Biochemistry (Jakubowski & Flatt), Gluconeogenesis

subquestions.stem: Gluconeogenesis is essentially:
options: Part of the citric acid cycle
Identical to glycolysis in every step
The reverse of glycolysis (with a few bypass steps)
subquestions.explanation: It runs opposite to glycolysis but uses different enzymes to bypass glycolysis's irreversible steps.

subquestions.stem: The organ most responsible for gluconeogenesis during fasting is the:
subquestions.explanation: The liver makes and exports glucose to maintain blood glucose (the kidney contributes during prolonged fasting).

items.stem: When blood glucose drops, glycogenolysis in the liver:
options: Breaks down stored glycogen to release glucose into the blood
items.explanation: Glycogenolysis is the breakdown of glycogen (via glycogen phosphorylase) to glucose-1-phosphate and ultimately glucose, which the liver releases to raise blood glucose. Building glycogen is glycogenesis, converting glucose to fat is lipogenesis, and making glucose from amino acids is gluconeogenesis.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry: Fundamentals of Biochemistry (Jakubowski & Flatt), Glycogen Metabolism

subquestions.stem: The enzyme that removes glucose units from glycogen is:
subquestions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase cleaves glucose-1-phosphate from glycogen; glycogen synthase builds glycogen.

subquestions.stem: Only the liver (not skeletal muscle) can release free glucose into the blood because it has:
subquestions.explanation: Glucose-6-phosphatase removes the phosphate so glucose can leave the cell; muscle lacks this enzyme.

items.stem: After a carbohydrate-rich meal, blood glucose rises and the pancreas secretes insulin. Insulin promotes:
options: Glucose uptake by cells and storage as glycogen and fat (an anabolic, glucose-lowering response)
Breakdown of glycogen and release of glucose into the blood
items.explanation: Insulin is the anabolic, glucose-lowering hormone: it stimulates glucose uptake (e.g., via GLUT4), glycogen synthesis, and fat storage. Glycogen breakdown, gluconeogenesis, and ketone production are promoted by glucagon (and other counter-regulatory hormones) when blood glucose is low, the opposite of insulin's action.

source.name: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e ch.17 (Endocrine System: pancreas)

subquestions.stem: Glucagon is released when blood glucose is:
subquestions.explanation: Glucagon opposes insulin, mobilizing glucose when blood levels fall.

subquestions.stem: Insulin and glucagon are secreted by which organ?
subquestions.explanation: Beta cells make insulin and alpha cells make glucagon in the pancreatic islets.

items.stem: The pentose phosphate pathway (hexose monophosphate shunt) is important mainly because it produces:
options: NADPH for biosynthesis and antioxidant defense, and ribose-5-phosphate for nucleotides
Large amounts of ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation
FADH2 for the electron transport chain
items.explanation: The pentose phosphate pathway generates NADPH (used in reductive biosynthesis such as fatty acid synthesis and in reducing glutathione for antioxidant defense) and ribose-5-phosphate (a precursor for nucleotide synthesis). It is not a major ATP producer, does not generate FADH2, and does not make lactate.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry: Fundamentals of Biochemistry (Jakubowski & Flatt), Pentose Phosphate Pathway

subquestions.stem: NADPH differs from NADH in that NADPH is used primarily for:
subquestions.explanation: NADPH supplies reducing power for building molecules, whereas NADH feeds the ETC for ATP.

subquestions.stem: The pentose phosphate pathway occurs in the:
subquestions.explanation: Like glycolysis, the pathway is cytosolic.

items.stem: Beta-oxidation of fatty acids takes place in the mitochondrial matrix and directly produces:
options: Glucose that is exported to the blood
Acetyl-CoA units plus NADH and FADH2, which feed the citric acid cycle and ETC
items.explanation: Beta-oxidation sequentially cleaves two-carbon acetyl-CoA units from fatty acids, generating NADH and FADH2 in each round; the acetyl-CoA enters the citric acid cycle and the reduced carriers feed the ETC, which is why fats yield abundant ATP per gram. It does not make glucose or amino acids, and its ATP largely comes from oxidative phosphorylation, not substrate-level phosphorylation.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry: Fundamentals of Biochemistry (Jakubowski & Flatt), Fatty Acid Oxidation

subquestions.stem: Each round of beta-oxidation shortens the fatty acid chain by how many carbons?
subquestions.explanation: Each cycle removes a two-carbon acetyl-CoA unit.

subquestions.stem: Why do fats provide more energy per gram than carbohydrates?
options: Fatty acids are more reduced, yielding more electrons for the ETC
Fats bypass the citric acid cycle
Fats are broken down in the cytoplasm
subquestions.explanation: Highly reduced hydrocarbon chains release more high-energy electrons when oxidized.

items.stem: During prolonged fasting or very low carbohydrate intake, the liver produces ketone bodies. These are made from:
options: Amino acids converted directly into glucose
Acetyl-CoA (derived largely from fatty acid oxidation) and serve as fuel for the brain and heart
Glucose that the brain cannot use
Lactate produced by red blood cells
items.explanation: When carbohydrate is scarce, excess acetyl-CoA from fatty acid oxidation is converted by the liver into ketone bodies (acetoacetate and beta-hydroxybutyrate), which are exported as an alternative fuel that the brain and heart can oxidize. Ketones are not made from glucose, are distinct from gluconeogenesis (amino acids to glucose), and are not derived from lactate.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry: Fundamentals of Biochemistry (Jakubowski & Flatt), Ketone Body Metabolism

subquestions.stem: Ketone bodies are synthesized primarily in the:
subquestions.explanation: The liver makes ketone bodies and exports them; it cannot use them itself (it lacks the enzyme to reactivate them).

subquestions.stem: During prolonged starvation, ketone bodies become an important fuel for the:
subquestions.explanation: The brain adapts to use ketone bodies, sparing glucose; red blood cells still rely on glucose (they lack mitochondria).

items.stem: Which statement is a core tenet of the cell theory?
options: The cell is not the basic unit of life
Cells arise spontaneously from nonliving matter
Only multicellular organisms are made of cells
All living organisms are composed of one or more cells, and all cells arise from pre-existing cells
items.explanation: The cell theory holds that all living things are made of cells, the cell is the basic unit of life, and all cells come from pre-existing cells. Spontaneous generation of cells from nonliving matter was disproven, unicellular organisms are also made of cells (so 'only multicellular organisms' is false), and the cell is indeed the fundamental unit of life.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e ch.4 (Cell Structure)

subquestions.stem: The idea that new cells come only from existing cells refutes:
