options: Emulsifying large fat globules into smaller droplets
subquestions.explanation: Bile salts emulsify fats, increasing surface area for lipase to act; bile is not itself an enzyme.

subquestions.stem: The pancreas contributes to digestion by secreting:
options: Digestive enzymes and bicarbonate into the small intestine
subquestions.explanation: The exocrine pancreas releases enzymes (amylase, lipase, proteases) and bicarbonate to neutralize acid.

items.stem: In the nephron, the initial filtration of blood, forming a filtrate low in large proteins and cells, occurs at the:
items.explanation: Blood is filtered under pressure at the glomerulus (a capillary tuft) into Bowman's capsule, producing a filtrate that normally excludes large proteins and blood cells. The loop of Henle concentrates the filtrate, and the distal tubule and collecting duct fine-tune reabsorption and secretion; none of these is the site of initial filtration.

source.name: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e ch.25 (The Urinary System: the nephron)

subquestions.stem: Most reabsorption of glucose, amino acids, and water occurs in the:
subquestions.explanation: The proximal tubule reclaims the bulk of filtered nutrients and water back into the blood.

subquestions.stem: Finding glucose in the urine most likely indicates that:
options: The loop of Henle is too long
Blood glucose exceeded the reabsorption capacity of the tubules
subquestions.explanation: When filtered glucose exceeds the transport maximum (as in diabetes), the excess spills into urine.

items.stem: The loop of Henle helps the kidney produce concentrated urine. In the ascending limb of the loop:
options: Glucose is secreted into the filtrate
Both salt and water are freely reabsorbed together
Water freely leaves while salt is trapped inside
Salt (NaCl) is pumped out while the limb remains impermeable to water, contributing to the medullary osmotic gradient
items.explanation: The ascending limb actively transports NaCl out of the filtrate but is impermeable to water, which concentrates the surrounding medulla and dilutes the filtrate; the water-permeable descending limb then loses water to this gradient. This countercurrent multiplier lets the collecting duct reabsorb water (under ADH). The other options misassign the limbs' permeabilities or invoke glucose secretion, which does not occur here.

source.name: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e ch.25 (The Urinary System: countercurrent multiplier)

subquestions.stem: The DESCENDING limb of the loop of Henle is permeable mainly to:
subquestions.explanation: Water leaves the water-permeable descending limb into the salty medulla, concentrating the filtrate.

subquestions.stem: ADH increases urine concentration by making the ____ more permeable to water.
subquestions.explanation: ADH inserts aquaporins in the collecting duct so water is reabsorbed down the medullary gradient.

items.stem: Which statement correctly contrasts innate and adaptive immunity?
options: Both are equally slow and both generate long-term memory
Innate immunity is a rapid, nonspecific first line of defense; adaptive immunity is slower, highly specific, and generates memory
Adaptive immunity is the body's first and fastest response
Innate immunity is specific and has memory; adaptive immunity is nonspecific
items.explanation: Innate immunity (skin, phagocytes, inflammation, complement) responds quickly and nonspecifically to any pathogen, whereas adaptive immunity (lymphocytes) develops more slowly, targets specific antigens, and forms immunological memory for faster future responses. The other options reverse these properties.

source.name: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e ch.21 (The Lymphatic and Immune System)

subquestions.stem: Immunological 'memory' that makes a second exposure to a pathogen faster and stronger is a feature of:
subquestions.explanation: Memory B and T cells persist after an infection, enabling a rapid secondary response.

subquestions.stem: Which is an example of an innate immune defense?
options: Phagocytosis by macrophages and the skin barrier
Cytotoxic T-cell killing of a specific infected cell
subquestions.explanation: Phagocytes and physical barriers act nonspecifically as innate defenses; the others are adaptive.

items.stem: Humoral immunity is mediated primarily by:
options: Red blood cells, which engulf antigens
Cytotoxic T cells, which directly kill infected body cells
B cells, which produce antibodies against extracellular pathogens and toxins
items.explanation: Humoral (antibody-mediated) immunity relies on B cells (plasma cells) secreting antibodies that neutralize and tag extracellular pathogens and toxins. Cytotoxic T cells drive cell-mediated immunity by killing infected cells, red blood cells carry oxygen (not immune function), and platelets are for clotting, not antibody production.

source.name: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e ch.21 (The Adaptive Immune Response)

subquestions.stem: Helper T cells are important because they:
options: Coordinate the immune response by activating B cells and cytotoxic T cells
subquestions.explanation: Helper T cells release signals that stimulate both humoral and cell-mediated arms of immunity.

subquestions.stem: Antibodies are proteins also known as:
subquestions.explanation: Antibodies (immunoglobulins) bind specific antigens; interferons/cytokines are signaling molecules.

items.stem: According to the sliding filament theory of muscle contraction, a sarcomere shortens when:
options: Myosin heads bind actin and pull the thin filaments toward the center of the sarcomere
Calcium is removed from the muscle cell
The actin and myosin filaments themselves shorten in length
items.explanation: In the sliding filament model, myosin cross-bridges attach to actin and pull the thin filaments inward, so the filaments slide past each other and the sarcomere shortens without the filaments themselves changing length. Contraction is triggered by Ca2+ (not its removal), and the Z-lines move closer together rather than dissolving.

source.name: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e ch.10 (Muscle Tissue: the sarcomere)

subquestions.stem: Muscle contraction is triggered when calcium binds:
options: Troponin, moving tropomyosin off the actin binding sites
subquestions.explanation: Ca2+ binds troponin, shifting tropomyosin to expose actin's myosin-binding sites.

subquestions.stem: The energy for the myosin power stroke and cross-bridge cycling comes from:
subquestions.explanation: ATP powers myosin head movement and its release from actin during each cycle.

items.stem: Which description correctly matches a muscle type?
options: Cardiac muscle is striated, involuntary, and has intercalated discs that link cells
Cardiac muscle is nonstriated and voluntary
Smooth muscle is striated and voluntary
Skeletal muscle is nonstriated and involuntary
items.explanation: Cardiac muscle is striated and involuntary, and its cells are joined by intercalated discs (with gap junctions) that synchronize contraction. Skeletal muscle is striated and voluntary, and smooth muscle is nonstriated and involuntary, so the other options mismatch the properties.

source.name: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e ch.10 (Muscle Tissue: types of muscle)

subquestions.stem: Which muscle type is under voluntary (conscious) control?
subquestions.explanation: Skeletal muscle moves the skeleton under voluntary control; cardiac and smooth are involuntary.

subquestions.stem: Intercalated discs in cardiac muscle allow the cells to:
options: Contract in a coordinated, synchronized manner
subquestions.explanation: Gap junctions at intercalated discs let electrical signals spread cell-to-cell for a unified beat.

items.stem: Bone is continually remodeled throughout life. Which pairing of bone cell and function is correct?
options: Osteocytes actively divide to form new bone daily
Osteoblasts resorb bone; osteoclasts build bone
Both osteoblasts and osteoclasts only store fat
Osteoclasts break down (resorb) bone; osteoblasts build new bone matrix
items.explanation: Osteoclasts resorb bone (breaking down matrix and releasing calcium), while osteoblasts synthesize new bone matrix; their balance governs remodeling and calcium homeostasis. The reversed roles are incorrect, these cells are not fat-storage cells, and osteocytes are mature bone cells that maintain the matrix rather than dividing to form new bone.

source.name: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e ch.6 (Bone Tissue and the Skeletal System)

subquestions.stem: A helpful memory aid is that osteoBlasts:
subquestions.explanation: Osteoblasts Build; osteoClasts Consume/resorb bone.

subquestions.stem: Bone serves as the body's main reservoir for which mineral?
subquestions.explanation: Bone stores most of the body's calcium, released or deposited during remodeling under hormonal control.

items.stem: During the menstrual cycle, a mid-cycle surge of which hormone directly triggers ovulation?
items.explanation: A sharp surge in luteinizing hormone (LH), stimulated by rising estrogen, triggers ovulation (release of the egg from the mature follicle). Progesterone rises after ovulation (from the corpus luteum) to maintain the uterine lining, ADH regulates water balance, and prolactin stimulates milk production; none of these triggers ovulation.

source.name: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e ch.27 (The Reproductive System: the ovarian cycle)

subquestions.stem: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) is named for its role in:
subquestions.explanation: FSH promotes maturation of follicles, which then secrete estrogen.

subquestions.stem: After ovulation, the corpus luteum secretes progesterone mainly to:
options: Maintain and prepare the uterine lining (endometrium)
subquestions.explanation: Progesterone sustains the endometrium for possible implantation; its fall triggers menstruation.

items.stem: When body temperature rises above its set point, the hypothalamus initiates responses such as sweating and dilation of skin blood vessels. This is an example of:
options: An endocrine reflex unrelated to temperature
Positive feedback that raises temperature further
Negative feedback restoring homeostasis by promoting heat loss
items.explanation: Sweating (evaporative cooling) and cutaneous vasodilation increase heat loss to bring temperature back toward the set point, a classic negative feedback loop for thermoregulation. It opposes the rise rather than amplifying it (so not positive feedback), represents functioning homeostasis, and is directly temperature-related.

source.name: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e ch.1 (An Introduction to the Human Body: homeostasis)

subquestions.stem: When the body is too COLD, a homeostatic response is to:
options: Sweat and dilate skin blood vessels
Shiver and constrict skin blood vessels to conserve heat
Increase urine output to warm up
subquestions.explanation: Shivering generates heat and vasoconstriction reduces heat loss, opposing the drop in temperature.

subquestions.stem: The main integrating center for temperature regulation is the:
subquestions.explanation: The hypothalamus acts as the body's thermostat, comparing temperature to the set point.

items.stem: When blood calcium falls too low, the parathyroid glands release parathyroid hormone (PTH), which acts to:
options: Raise blood calcium by stimulating bone resorption and increasing renal calcium reabsorption
Lower blood calcium by depositing it into bone
items.explanation: PTH raises blood calcium by stimulating osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, increasing calcium reabsorption in the kidney, and promoting activation of vitamin D (boosting intestinal calcium absorption). Depositing calcium into bone to lower blood calcium is the action of calcitonin, PTH definitely affects calcium, and hormones do not transmute calcium into sodium.

source.name: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e ch.17 (The Endocrine System: calcium homeostasis)

subquestions.stem: The hormone that opposes PTH by LOWERING blood calcium is:
subquestions.explanation: Calcitonin (from the thyroid) promotes calcium deposition into bone, lowering blood calcium.

subquestions.stem: PTH and calcitonin acting in opposition to keep blood calcium stable is an example of:
subquestions.explanation: Two hormones with opposite effects finely regulate a variable around its set point.

items.stem: The skin (integumentary system) contributes to homeostasis and defense mainly by:
options: Producing digestive enzymes for the gut
Acting as a physical barrier against pathogens and helping regulate body temperature and water loss
Generating action potentials to move muscles
Filtering the blood to form urine
items.explanation: The skin forms a protective barrier against pathogens and physical damage, limits water loss, and helps regulate temperature (through sweat glands and blood-vessel dilation/constriction). It does not produce digestive enzymes, generate action potentials for movement, or filter blood to make urine, which are roles of the digestive, nervous/muscular, and urinary systems.

source.name: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e ch.5 (The Integumentary System)

subquestions.stem: The skin also participates in the synthesis of which vitamin when exposed to sunlight?
subquestions.explanation: UV light drives the first step of vitamin D synthesis in the skin.

subquestions.stem: As part of INNATE immunity, intact skin functions as a:
subquestions.explanation: Unbroken skin blocks pathogen entry, a nonspecific innate defense.

items.stem: A ball is released from rest and falls freely near Earth's surface for 3.0 s. Ignoring air resistance and taking g = 10 m/s², what is its speed just before it lands?
items.explanation: For free fall from rest, v = v₀ + g t = 0 + (10)(3.0) = 30 m/s. Distractors: 10 m/s uses only g (ignores t); 45 m/s is the distance fallen ½ g t² = ½(10)(9.0) = 45 m mislabeled as a speed; 90 m/s comes from g t² = (10)(9.0).

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 2: Kinematics

subquestions.stem: For an object dropped from rest, which equation gives speed after time t?
subquestions.explanation: With v₀ = 0 and a = g, v = v₀ + a t = g t. The expression ½ g t² is the distance fallen, not the speed.

subquestions.stem: Evaluate v = g t with g = 10 m/s² and t = 3.0 s.
The quantity ½ g t² = ½(10)(3.0)² = 45 m represents what?
subquestions.explanation: ½ g t² is the vertical displacement (45 m), which is why '45 m/s' is a trap answer.

items.stem: A marble rolls off the edge of a table 1.25 m high with a horizontal speed of 3.0 m/s. Taking g = 10 m/s² and neglecting air resistance, how far from the base of the table does it land?
items.explanation: Vertical and horizontal motions are independent. Time to fall: h = ½ g t² → t = √(2h/g) = √(2·1.25/10) = √0.25 = 0.50 s. Horizontal range: x = vₓ t = (3.0)(0.50) = 1.5 m. Distractors use wrong fall times (t = 0.25 s → 0.75 m; t = 1.0 s → 3.0 m; t = 2.0 s → 6.0 m).

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 3: Two-Dimensional Kinematics

subquestions.stem: With no air resistance, the marble's horizontal velocity during flight...
subquestions.explanation: There is no horizontal force, so aₓ = 0 and vₓ is constant.

subquestions.stem: How long is the marble airborne? Use h = ½ g t² with h = 1.25 m, g = 10 m/s².
subquestions.explanation: t = √(2h/g) = √0.25 = 0.50 s. Time aloft depends only on the vertical drop, not the horizontal speed.

subquestions.stem: Using t = 0.50 s and vₓ = 3.0 m/s, the horizontal distance is...

items.stem: A constant net force gives a 2.0 kg cart an acceleration of 3.0 m/s². What is the magnitude of the net force?
items.explanation: Newton's second law: F_net = m a = (2.0 kg)(3.0 m/s²) = 6.0 N. Distractors: 1.5 N divides a/m; 5.0 N adds m + a; 12 N doubles the result.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 4: Dynamics: Newton's Laws of Motion

subquestions.stem: Newton's second law relates net force, mass, and acceleration as:
subquestions.explanation: Force equals mass times acceleration.

subquestions.stem: Compute (2.0 kg)(3.0 m/s²).
If the same net force acted on a 4.0 kg cart instead, its acceleration would be:
subquestions.explanation: a = F/m = 6.0/4.0 = 1.5 m/s². Doubling the mass halves the acceleration for a fixed force.

items.stem: A 10 kg crate slides across a level floor. If the coefficient of kinetic friction is 0.30, what horizontal force keeps it moving at constant velocity? (g = 10 m/s²)
items.explanation: Constant velocity → a = 0 → applied force = kinetic friction. On level ground N = mg = (10)(10) = 100 N, so f_k = μ_k N = (0.30)(100) = 30 N. Distractors: 3.0 N forgets g; 100 N is the normal force (forgot to multiply by μ_k); 300 N applies μ_k to a mis-scaled weight.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 5: Further Applications of Newton's Laws: Friction

subquestions.stem: For a crate on a horizontal floor, the normal force equals:
subquestions.explanation: Vertical equilibrium gives N = mg.

subquestions.stem: Kinetic friction is calculated as:
subquestions.explanation: Kinetic friction equals the coefficient times the normal force.

subquestions.stem: At constant velocity, the applied horizontal force compared with friction is:
subquestions.explanation: Zero acceleration means the horizontal forces balance exactly.

items.stem: A block is released from rest on a frictionless incline angled 30° above the horizontal. What is its acceleration down the incline? (g = 10 m/s²)
items.explanation: On a frictionless incline, a = g sinθ = (10)(sin 30°) = (10)(0.50) = 5.0 m/s². Distractors: 8.7 m/s² = g cosθ (the perpendicular component, which sets the normal force); 10 m/s² is g itself (only for vertical free fall); 2.5 m/s² halves the correct value.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 4: Newton's Laws applied to an incline

subquestions.stem: The component of gravity along a frictionless incline of angle θ is:
subquestions.explanation: The along-incline component is g sinθ; the perpendicular component is g cosθ.

subquestions.stem: The perpendicular component g cosθ primarily determines the:
subquestions.explanation: N = mg cosθ; it does not drive motion along a frictionless ramp.

items.stem: On a seesaw pivoted at its center, a 40 kg child sits 1.5 m from the pivot. How far from the pivot on the other side must a 60 kg child sit to balance it?
items.explanation: Rotational equilibrium requires equal and opposite torques: m₁ g d₁ = m₂ g d₂ → (40)(1.5) = (60)(d₂) → d₂ = 60/60 = 1.0 m. The heavier child sits closer. Distractors: 1.5 m ignores the mass difference; 2.25 m = 1.5 × 1.5; 0.5 m over-corrects.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 9: Statics and Torque

subquestions.stem: Torque about a pivot equals:
subquestions.explanation: Torque τ = F × (perpendicular lever-arm distance).

subquestions.stem: For the seesaw to balance, the two torques must be:
options: equal in magnitude, opposite in sense
subquestions.explanation: Net torque must be zero, so the torques cancel.

subquestions.stem: To balance a lighter child, the heavier child must sit:
subquestions.explanation: A larger force needs a shorter lever arm to give the same torque.

items.stem: How much work is done lifting a 5.0 kg box vertically 2.0 m at constant speed? (g = 10 m/s²)
items.explanation: At constant speed the applied force equals the weight mg. W = F d = m g h = (5.0)(10)(2.0) = 100 J. Distractors drop a factor (mg alone, or mh) or add an extra power of ten.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 7: Work, Energy, and Energy Resources

subquestions.stem: Work done by a constant force along the direction of motion is:
subquestions.explanation: Work = force × distance moved along the force.

subquestions.stem: The upward force needed to raise the box at constant speed equals:
subquestions.explanation: Constant speed → no acceleration → applied force balances the weight mg.

subquestions.stem: Evaluate (5.0 kg)(10 m/s²)(2.0 m).

items.stem: A 1000 kg car travels at 20 m/s. What is its kinetic energy?
items.explanation: KE = ½ m v² = ½(1000)(20²) = ½(1000)(400) = 2.0×10⁵ J. Common errors: forgetting to square v gives ½(1000)(20) = 1.0×10⁴ J; dropping the ½ gives 4.0×10⁵ J.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 7: Kinetic Energy and the Work-Energy Theorem

subquestions.stem: Kinetic energy is:
subquestions.explanation: KE = ½ m v².

subquestions.stem: Evaluate 20².
If the car's speed doubled, its kinetic energy would become:
subquestions.explanation: KE ∝ v², so doubling v quadruples KE.

items.stem: A 2.0 kg ball is released from rest at a height of 5.0 m on a frictionless track. What is its speed at the bottom? (g = 10 m/s²)
items.explanation: Energy conservation: m g h = ½ m v² → v = √(2 g h) = √(2·10·5) = √100 = 10 m/s. Mass cancels. Distractors: 7.1 m/s = √50 (used g h instead of 2 g h); 100 m/s is 2 g h left un-rooted.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 7: Conservation of Energy

subquestions.stem: As the ball descends a frictionless track, gravitational PE converts mainly into:
subquestions.explanation: With no friction, PE → KE.

subquestions.stem: Solving m g h = ½ m v² for v gives:
subquestions.explanation: Mass cancels; v = √(2 g h).

subquestions.stem: Does the answer depend on the 2.0 kg mass?
subquestions.explanation: Mass appears on both sides and cancels.

items.stem: A motor lifts a 100 kg load straight up at a constant speed of 2.0 m/s. What is the motor's mechanical power output? (g = 10 m/s²)
items.explanation: Power P = F v. At constant speed the force equals the weight: F = mg = (100)(10) = 1000 N. P = (1000)(2.0) = 2000 W. Distractors: 200 W forgets g; 20000 W adds an extra factor of ten.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 7: Power

subquestions.stem: Power delivered by a force moving its point of application at velocity v is:
subquestions.explanation: P = F v (equivalently, work per unit time).

subquestions.stem: The force needed to raise the load at constant speed is:
Evaluate (1000 N)(2.0 m/s).

items.stem: A 0.15 kg baseball moving at 40 m/s is caught and brought to rest in 0.010 s. What is the average force the glove exerts on the ball?
items.explanation: Impulse-momentum theorem: F Δt = Δp = m Δv = (0.15)(40) = 6.0 kg·m/s. F = Δp/Δt = 6.0/0.010 = 600 N. Distractors differ by powers of ten (decimal/unit slips).

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 8: Linear Momentum and Collisions

subquestions.stem: The impulse-momentum theorem states:
subquestions.explanation: Impulse (F Δt) equals the change in momentum (m Δv).

subquestions.stem: The magnitude of the ball's change in momentum is:
For a fixed change in momentum, a longer contact time produces:
subquestions.explanation: F = Δp/Δt; increasing Δt lowers F (the principle behind airbags and 'following through').

items.stem: A 0.50 kg mass on a spring with force constant k = 200 N/m oscillates in simple harmonic motion. What is the period of oscillation?
items.explanation: T = 2π√(m/k) = 2π√(0.50/200) = 2π√(0.0025) = 2π(0.050) = 0.31 s. The period is independent of amplitude. Distractors: 6.3 s = 2π with the √(m/k) factor mishandled; 3.1 s = π.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 16: Oscillatory Motion and Waves

subquestions.stem: For a mass-spring oscillator, the period is:
subquestions.explanation: Larger mass → longer period; stiffer spring → shorter period: T = 2π√(m/k).

subquestions.stem: If the mass were quadrupled to 2.0 kg, the period would:
subquestions.explanation: T ∝ √m, so ×4 mass → ×2 period.

items.stem: An object has a mass of 240 g and a volume of 30 cm³. What is its density?
items.explanation: Density ρ = m/V = 240 g / 30 cm³ = 8.0 g/cm³. Distractors: 0.125 inverts to V/m; 270 adds m + V; 80 misplaces a decimal.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 11: Fluid Statics (Density)

subquestions.stem: Density is defined as:
subquestions.explanation: Density = mass per unit volume.

subquestions.stem: Evaluate 240 g ÷ 30 cm³.
Relative to water (1.0 g/cm³), the specific gravity of this object is:
subquestions.explanation: Specific gravity = ρ_object / ρ_water = 8.0/1.0 = 8.0 (dimensionless).

items.stem: A block floats in water with 90% of its volume submerged. What is the density of the block? (ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³)
items.explanation: For a floating object, the fraction of volume submerged equals the ratio of densities: ρ_object/ρ_fluid = 0.90, so ρ_object = 0.90 × 1000 = 900 kg/m³. A density of 1000 would float fully submerged (neutral); 1100 would sink.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 11: Archimedes' Principle

subquestions.stem: Archimedes' principle: the buoyant force equals the weight of the:
subquestions.explanation: Buoyant force = weight of displaced fluid.

subquestions.stem: A freely floating object displaces a weight of fluid equal to:
subquestions.explanation: Floating means equilibrium: buoyant force = the object's weight.

subquestions.stem: If an object's density exceeds the fluid's density, the object will:
subquestions.explanation: Denser-than-fluid → weight exceeds maximum buoyant force → it sinks.

items.stem: What is the gauge pressure due to the water at a depth of 10 m in a freshwater lake? (ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 10 m/s²)
items.explanation: Gauge pressure P = ρ g h = (1000)(10)(10) = 1.0×10⁵ Pa (≈ 1 atm). This adds to atmospheric pressure to give absolute pressure. Distractors drop or add a factor of ten.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 11: Variation of Pressure with Depth

subquestions.stem: Hydrostatic (gauge) pressure at depth h is:
subquestions.explanation: Pressure increases linearly with depth: P = ρ g h.

subquestions.stem: Does the pressure at a given depth depend on the container's shape or width?
options: no — only depth, density, and g
subquestions.explanation: Hydrostatic pressure depends only on depth, fluid density, and g (the hydrostatic paradox).

items.stem: In a hydraulic lift, 20 N is applied to a small piston of area 0.010 m² and transmitted to a large piston of area 0.50 m². What force appears at the large piston?
items.explanation: Pascal's principle: pressure is transmitted undiminished, so F₁/A₁ = F₂/A₂ → F₂ = F₁(A₂/A₁) = 20 × (0.50/0.010) = 20 × 50 = 1000 N. Distractor 0.40 N uses the inverse area ratio.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 11: Pascal's Principle

subquestions.stem: Pascal's principle states that a pressure change applied to an enclosed fluid is:
subquestions.explanation: The same pressure change appears everywhere in the enclosed fluid.

subquestions.stem: The area ratio A₂/A₁ = 0.50/0.010 equals:
subquestions.explanation: 0.50 / 0.010 = 50, the force-multiplication factor.

subquestions.stem: A hydraulic lift multiplies force, but what is conserved?
subquestions.explanation: The large piston moves a proportionally smaller distance, so work in ≈ work out.

items.stem: An incompressible fluid flows through a pipe that narrows so its cross-sectional area is halved. Compared with the wide section, the fluid speed in the narrow section is:
items.explanation: Continuity: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ (volume flow rate is constant). If A is halved, v must double so the product is unchanged.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 12: Flow Rate and Its Relation to Velocity

subquestions.stem: For incompressible flow, the continuity equation states:
subquestions.explanation: Volume flow rate Q = A v is conserved along the pipe.

subquestions.stem: If cross-sectional area decreases, the fluid speed must:
subquestions.explanation: To keep A v constant, smaller A requires larger v.

subquestions.stem: Halving the area changes the speed by a factor of:
subquestions.explanation: v₂ = v₁(A₁/A₂) = v₁(2) → twice as fast.

items.stem: According to Bernoulli's principle, where a horizontal fluid streamline speeds up, the pressure it exerts on the walls:
items.explanation: Bernoulli's equation (P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant) at constant height means higher speed accompanies lower pressure. This underlies airfoil lift and the pressure drop in constricted vessels.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 12: Bernoulli's Equation

subquestions.stem: Bernoulli's equation expresses conservation of:
subquestions.explanation: It is an energy-per-unit-volume balance for flowing fluid.

subquestions.stem: At constant height, if fluid speed increases, its pressure must:
subquestions.explanation: The ½ρv² term rises, so the P term must fall to keep the sum constant.

subquestions.stem: In a narrowed blood vessel the fluid speeds up, so lateral pressure on the wall there:
subquestions.explanation: Higher speed at the constriction lowers lateral pressure.

items.stem: Blood flow through a vessel follows Poiseuille's law, in which volume flow rate is proportional to the fourth power of the radius. If a vessel's radius is reduced to half its original value (pressure gradient unchanged), the volume flow rate becomes:
items.explanation: Poiseuille's law: Q = πΔP r⁴ / (8ηL), so Q ∝ r⁴. Halving r gives (½)⁴ = 1/16 of the flow. This steep dependence is why modest vessel narrowing sharply reduces perfusion.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 12: Viscosity and Laminar Flow; Poiseuille's Law

subquestions.stem: In Poiseuille flow, volume flow rate depends on the vessel radius as:
subquestions.explanation: Q ∝ r⁴ — a very strong dependence.

subquestions.stem: Evaluate (1/2)⁴.
Poiseuille's law describes which flow regime?
subquestions.explanation: It applies to steady laminar flow of a viscous fluid.

items.stem: What volume does 2.0 mol of an ideal gas occupy at STP (273 K, 1.0 atm)? (R = 0.0821 L·atm·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹)
items.explanation: The molar volume of an ideal gas at STP is 22.4 L/mol, so 2.0 mol occupies 44.8 L. Equivalently V = nRT/P = (2.0)(0.0821)(273)/1.0 ≈ 44.8 L. Distractors correspond to 0.5, 1.0, and 4.0 mol.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 9: Gases — The Ideal Gas Law

subquestions.stem: The ideal gas law is:
subquestions.explanation: PV = nRT relates pressure, volume, moles, and temperature.

subquestions.stem: The molar volume of an ideal gas at STP is approximately:
subquestions.explanation: One mole of ideal gas occupies about 22.4 L at 273 K and 1 atm.

subquestions.stem: At fixed T and P, doubling the number of moles changes the volume by:
subquestions.explanation: V ∝ n (Avogadro's principle).

items.stem: A gas mixture contains 3.0 mol N₂ and 1.0 mol O₂ at a total pressure of 800 mmHg. What is the partial pressure of O₂?
items.explanation: Partial pressure = mole fraction × total pressure. X(O₂) = 1.0/(3.0+1.0) = 0.25, so P(O₂) = 0.25 × 800 = 200 mmHg (N₂ supplies the other 600 mmHg).

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 9: Gases — Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures

subquestions.stem: Dalton's law: the total pressure of a mixture equals:
options: the sum of the partial pressures
subquestions.explanation: Total pressure is the sum of each component's partial pressure.

subquestions.stem: The mole fraction of O₂ is:
Partial pressure of a component equals:
subquestions.explanation: P_i = X_i × P_total.

items.stem: At the same temperature, how does the average (rms) speed of helium atoms (M = 4 g/mol) compare with that of oxygen molecules (M = 32 g/mol)?
items.explanation: By kinetic-molecular theory, at a given temperature all gases share the same average kinetic energy, so v_rms ∝ 1/√M. The He:O₂ speed ratio is √(32/4) = √8 ≈ 2.83, so helium is ~2.8× faster (Graham's law).

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 9: The Kinetic-Molecular Theory; Effusion

subquestions.stem: At a given temperature, all ideal gases have the same average:
subquestions.explanation: Average translational KE depends only on temperature.

subquestions.stem: Root-mean-square speed depends on molar mass as:
subquestions.explanation: Setting ½m v² equal for all gases gives v_rms ∝ 1/√M.

subquestions.stem: Evaluate √(32/4) = √8.

items.stem: According to Henry's law, if the partial pressure of a gas above a liquid is tripled at constant temperature, the equilibrium concentration of that gas dissolved in the liquid will:
items.explanation: Henry's law: C = k·P — the amount of gas dissolved is directly proportional to its partial pressure. Tripling the partial pressure triples the dissolved concentration. This governs O₂/CO₂ loading in blood and explains decompression sickness.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 11: Solutions and Colloids — Henry's Law

subquestions.stem: Henry's law relates dissolved gas concentration to the gas's:
subquestions.explanation: Dissolved concentration is proportional to partial pressure.

subquestions.stem: Raising the temperature generally makes a gas's solubility in a liquid:
subquestions.explanation: Gas solubility typically falls as temperature rises (warm soda goes flat).

subquestions.stem: Henry's law is most directly relevant to which physiological process?
subquestions.explanation: It underlies O₂ and CO₂ exchange between alveolar air and blood.

items.stem: Two point charges are separated by a distance r. If the distance between them is doubled, the electrostatic force between them becomes:
items.explanation: Coulomb's law: F = k q₁ q₂ / r², so F ∝ 1/r². Doubling r multiplies the force by (1/2)² = 1/4. (Force depends on the square of distance but only linearly on each charge.)

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 18: Electric Charge and Coulomb's Law

subquestions.stem: In Coulomb's law, force depends on the separation as:
subquestions.explanation: Electrostatic force follows an inverse-square law.

subquestions.stem: Doubling r multiplies 1/r² by:
If instead one of the charges were doubled (r fixed), the force would:
subquestions.explanation: F ∝ q₁ q₂, so doubling one charge doubles the force.

items.stem: What is the magnitude of the electric field 2.0 m from a point charge of +8.0×10⁻⁹ C? (k = 9.0×10⁹ N·m²/C²)
items.explanation: E = k q / r² = (9.0×10⁹)(8.0×10⁻⁹)/(2.0)² = 72/4 = 18 N/C. Distractors divide by r instead of r² (36) or make decimal errors.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 18: Electric Field

subquestions.stem: The electric field of a point charge is:
subquestions.explanation: Field strength falls off as 1/r².
9×8 = 72 and 10⁹×10⁻⁹ = 10⁰ = 1, so the product is 72.

subquestions.stem: Divide 72 by r² = (2.0)² = 4.

items.stem: A 12 V battery drives current through a 4.0 Ω resistor. What is the current?
items.explanation: Ohm's law V = I R → I = V/R = 12/4.0 = 3.0 A. Distractors: 0.33 A inverts to R/V; 48 A multiplies V×R.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 20: Ohm's Law: Resistance and Simple Circuits

subquestions.stem: Ohm's law is:
subquestions.explanation: Voltage = current × resistance.

subquestions.stem: Solve I = V/R with V = 12 V and R = 4.0 Ω.
If the resistance doubled at the same voltage, the current would:
subquestions.explanation: I ∝ 1/R at fixed V.

items.stem: Three resistors of 2.0 Ω, 3.0 Ω, and 5.0 Ω are connected in series. What is their equivalent resistance?
items.explanation: Series resistances add directly: R_eq = 2.0 + 3.0 + 5.0 = 10 Ω. (0.97 Ω is roughly the parallel combination; series resistors carry the same current and their voltages add.)

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 21: Resistors in Series and Parallel

subquestions.stem: For resistors in series, the equivalent resistance is:
options: the reciprocal of the sum of reciprocals
subquestions.explanation: In series, R_eq = R₁ + R₂ + R₃ + ...

subquestions.stem: In a series circuit, the current through each resistor is:
subquestions.explanation: A single path means one common current.

subquestions.stem: Evaluate 2.0 + 3.0 + 5.0.
subquestions.explanation: The sum is 10 Ω.

items.stem: Two 6.0 Ω resistors are connected in parallel. What is their equivalent resistance?
items.explanation: For parallel resistors, 1/R_eq = 1/6.0 + 1/6.0 = 2/6.0 = 1/3.0, so R_eq = 3.0 Ω. Two equal resistors in parallel give half of one. (12 Ω would be the series result.)

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 21: Resistors in Series and Parallel

subquestions.stem: For resistors in parallel, you add the:
Two equal resistors R in parallel give an equivalent resistance of:
subquestions.explanation: 1/R_eq = 2/R → R_eq = R/2.

subquestions.stem: In a parallel circuit, the voltage across each branch is:
subquestions.explanation: Parallel branches share the same two nodes, hence the same voltage.

items.stem: A resistor carries 2.0 A when connected across 12 V. What power does it dissipate?
items.explanation: P = I V = (2.0)(12) = 24 W. Equivalent forms are P = I²R and P = V²/R. Distractors: 6.0 = V/I; 14 = I+V; 144 = V² alone.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 20: Electric Power and Energy

subquestions.stem: Electrical power delivered to a device is:
subquestions.explanation: Power = current × voltage.

subquestions.stem: Compute (2.0 A)(12 V).
Which is an equivalent power formula (using Ohm's law)?
subquestions.explanation: Substituting V = IR into P = IV gives P = I²R (and P = V²/R).

items.stem: A capacitor stores 6.0×10⁻⁶ C of charge when 3.0 V is applied across it. What is its capacitance?
items.explanation: C = Q/V = (6.0×10⁻⁶ C)/(3.0 V) = 2.0×10⁻⁶ F = 2.0 μF. Distractors: 0.5 inverts to V/Q; 18 multiplies Q×V.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 19: Capacitors and Dielectrics

subquestions.stem: Capacitance is defined as:
subquestions.explanation: Capacitance = charge stored per volt applied.

subquestions.stem: Inserting a dielectric between the plates (charge fixed) does what to capacitance?
subquestions.explanation: A dielectric constant κ > 1 multiplies capacitance by κ.

items.subtopic: Magnetic force on a moving charge
items.stem: A charge moves parallel to a uniform magnetic field. The magnetic force on the charge is:
options: equal to qvB regardless of direction
items.explanation: The magnetic force is F = qvB sinθ. When v is parallel to B, θ = 0 and sinθ = 0, so F = 0. The force is maximum when v ⊥ B (θ = 90°) and is always perpendicular to both v and B.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 22: Magnetism — Force on a Moving Charge

subquestions.stem: In F = qvB sinθ, the angle θ is measured between:
subquestions.explanation: θ is the angle between the velocity and the magnetic field.
sin 0° = 0, so parallel motion gives no force.

subquestions.stem: A nonzero magnetic force on a moving charge points:
options: perpendicular to both v and B
subquestions.explanation: The force (qv × B) is perpendicular to both vectors, so it does no work and curves the path.

items.stem: A galvanic cell is built from Zn²⁺/Zn (E° = −0.76 V) and Cu²⁺/Cu (E° = +0.34 V). What is the standard cell potential?
items.explanation: The half-cell with the higher reduction potential (Cu²⁺/Cu) is the cathode; Zn is oxidized at the anode. E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode = 0.34 − (−0.76) = +1.10 V. A positive value means the reaction is spontaneous. (0.42 V comes from adding with a sign error.)

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 17: Electrochemistry — Standard Reduction Potentials

subquestions.stem: In a galvanic (voltaic) cell, oxidation occurs at the:
subquestions.explanation: Anode = oxidation; cathode = reduction (mnemonic: 'an ox, red cat').

subquestions.stem: Standard cell potential is calculated as:
subquestions.explanation: E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode (both as reduction potentials).

subquestions.stem: A positive E°cell indicates the reaction is:
subquestions.explanation: ΔG° = −nFE°cell, so E°cell > 0 gives ΔG° < 0 (spontaneous).

items.stem: During electrolysis, how many moles of electrons are required to deposit 1 mole of aluminum metal from Al³⁺?
items.explanation: The reduction half-reaction is Al³⁺ + 3 e⁻ → Al, so 3 moles of electrons (3 faradays) are needed per mole of Al. This stoichiometry is the basis of Faraday's laws of electrolysis.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 17: Electrochemistry — Electrolysis

subquestions.stem: In an electrolytic cell, metal is deposited (reduced) at the:
subquestions.explanation: Reduction always occurs at the cathode.

subquestions.stem: The reduction half-reaction for aluminum is:
subquestions.explanation: Al³⁺ gains 3 electrons to become neutral Al.

subquestions.stem: One faraday (≈ 96,485 C) corresponds to:
subquestions.explanation: A faraday is the charge of one mole of electrons.

items.stem: How much work is required to move a +2.0 C charge from a point at low potential to a point 5.0 V higher?
items.explanation: W = qΔV = (2.0 C)(5.0 V) = 10 J. Moving a positive charge to higher potential requires positive work (analogous to lifting a mass). Distractors: 0.4 = q/V; 2.5 = V/q.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 19: Electric Potential Energy: Potential Difference

subquestions.stem: The relationship between energy, charge, and voltage is:
subquestions.explanation: Energy = charge × potential difference.

subquestions.stem: One volt is defined as:
Evaluate (2.0 C)(5.0 V).

items.stem: A wire has resistance R. It is replaced by a wire of the same material and length but twice the cross-sectional area. Its resistance becomes:
items.explanation: R = ρL/A: resistance is inversely proportional to cross-sectional area. Doubling A halves R. (Longer wires have more resistance; thicker wires have less.)

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 20: Resistance and Resistivity

subquestions.stem: The resistance of a uniform wire is:
subquestions.explanation: Resistance rises with length and resistivity, falls with area.

subquestions.stem: Resistance depends on cross-sectional area as:
Doubling the length instead (area fixed) would make the resistance:
subquestions.explanation: R ∝ L, so twice the length gives twice the resistance.

items.stem: A sound wave travels at 340 m/s with a frequency of 170 Hz. What is its wavelength?
items.explanation: The wave relation v = fλ gives λ = v/f = 340/170 = 2.0 m. Distractors invert the ratio or combine the numbers incorrectly.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 16: Waves

subquestions.stem: The relation among wave speed, frequency, and wavelength is:
subquestions.explanation: Speed = frequency × wavelength.

subquestions.stem: Solve λ = v/f with v = 340 m/s and f = 170 Hz.
If frequency increases while the wave speed stays fixed, the wavelength:
subquestions.explanation: λ ∝ 1/f at constant v.

items.stem: The sound intensity level at a location increases by 20 dB. By what factor did the sound intensity increase?
items.explanation: Sound level is β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀). A 20 dB rise means log₁₀(I/I₀) = 2, so I/I₀ = 10² = 100. Each 10 dB is a 10× change in intensity, so 20 dB is 10×10 = 100×. Distractors treat the scale as linear.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 17: Sound Intensity and Sound Level

subquestions.stem: The decibel scale for sound intensity is:
subquestions.explanation: Level in dB is proportional to the logarithm of intensity.

subquestions.stem: Each increase of 10 dB corresponds to the intensity multiplying by:
subquestions.explanation: 10 dB = one factor of 10 in intensity.

subquestions.stem: A 20 dB increase equals two 10 dB steps, a total factor of:

items.stem: An ambulance siren approaches a stationary observer. Compared with the frequency the siren emits, the frequency the observer hears is:
items.explanation: For a source approaching an observer, the wavefronts ahead are compressed, shortening the wavelength and raising the observed frequency (Doppler effect). Once the ambulance passes and recedes, the observed frequency drops below the emitted value.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 17: The Doppler Effect and Sonic Booms

subquestions.stem: The Doppler effect is a change in observed ______ caused by relative motion of source and observer.
subquestions.explanation: Relative motion shifts the perceived frequency (pitch).

subquestions.stem: When a source approaches, the wavefronts in front of it are:
subquestions.explanation: Compression shortens wavelength and raises frequency.

subquestions.stem: After the ambulance passes and moves away, the pitch you hear:
subquestions.explanation: A receding source gives a lower observed frequency.

items.stem: A string fixed at both ends has a fundamental frequency of 100 Hz. What is the frequency of its third harmonic?
items.explanation: A string fixed at both ends supports harmonics at integer multiples of the fundamental: f_n = n·f₁. The third harmonic is 3 × 100 = 300 Hz.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 16: Standing Waves and Resonance

subquestions.stem: For a string fixed at both ends, the allowed frequencies are:
subquestions.explanation: Both-ends-fixed (or both-ends-open) systems allow all integer harmonics.

subquestions.stem: The fundamental frequency corresponds to harmonic number:
subquestions.explanation: The fundamental is the first harmonic (n = 1).

subquestions.stem: Evaluate 3 × 100 Hz.
subquestions.explanation: The third harmonic is 300 Hz.

items.stem: Which type of electromagnetic radiation has the highest frequency?
items.explanation: Ordered by increasing frequency (and photon energy): radio < microwave < infrared < visible < ultraviolet < X-ray < gamma. Gamma rays have the highest frequency and shortest wavelength. All EM waves travel at c in vacuum.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 24: Electromagnetic Waves

subquestions.stem: All electromagnetic waves in a vacuum travel at:
subquestions.explanation: In vacuum every EM wave moves at c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s.

subquestions.stem: Higher-frequency EM radiation has a ______ wavelength.
subquestions.explanation: Since c = fλ, higher f means shorter λ.

subquestions.stem: Higher-frequency photons carry ______ energy (E = hf).
subquestions.explanation: Photon energy is proportional to frequency.

items.stem: Light passes from air (n = 1.0) into glass (n = 1.5). As it enters the glass, the light bends:
items.explanation: Snell's law: n₁ sinθ₁ = n₂ sinθ₂. Entering a higher-index medium, light slows (v = c/n) and bends toward the normal (θ₂ < θ₁). Going to a lower-index medium it bends away from the normal.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 25: Geometric Optics — The Law of Refraction

subquestions.stem: Snell's law of refraction is:
subquestions.explanation: The product n·sinθ is conserved across the interface.

subquestions.stem: As light enters a medium of higher refractive index, its speed:
subquestions.explanation: v = c/n, so larger n means slower light.

subquestions.stem: Entering a higher-index medium, the ray bends:
subquestions.explanation: Slowing down bends the ray toward the normal.

items.stem: Total internal reflection can occur when light travels:
options: from a lower-index to a higher-index medium at any angle
from a higher-index to a lower-index medium beyond the critical angle
items.explanation: Total internal reflection requires light going from a higher-index (denser) medium to a lower-index one AND an incidence angle above the critical angle (sinθ_c = n₂/n₁). It is the basis of fiber optics and endoscopes.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 25: Total Internal Reflection

subquestions.stem: For total internal reflection, light must travel from a medium of:
subquestions.explanation: Only going to a lower-index medium can the refracted ray disappear.

subquestions.stem: TIR requires the angle of incidence to be:
subquestions.explanation: Beyond the critical angle, no refracted ray escapes.

subquestions.stem: Which technology relies on total internal reflection?
subquestions.explanation: Light stays trapped in the fiber core by repeated TIR.

items.stem: An object is placed 30 cm from a converging (convex) lens with a focal length of 10 cm. Where is the image formed?
items.explanation: Thin-lens equation: 1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i → 1/d_i = 1/10 − 1/30 = 3/30 − 1/30 = 2/30 = 1/15, so d_i = 15 cm. The positive value means a real image on the opposite side.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 25: Image Formation by Lenses

subquestions.stem: The thin-lens equation is:
subquestions.explanation: The reciprocals of object and image distances sum to 1/f.

subquestions.stem: Compute 1/10 − 1/30.
Because the object lies beyond the focal length, the image is:
subquestions.explanation: A convex lens with d_o > f forms a real, inverted image.

items.stem: A concave mirror forms an image with magnification m = −2. This tells you the image is:
items.explanation: Magnification m = −d_i/d_o = h_i/h_o. The negative sign indicates an inverted image; the magnitude of 2 means it is twice as tall as the object (a real, enlarged image).

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 25: Image Formation by Mirrors

subquestions.stem: A negative magnification indicates the image is:
subquestions.explanation: Sign gives orientation; negative = inverted.

subquestions.stem: A magnification magnitude |m| = 2 means the image height is:
subquestions.explanation: |m| is the ratio of image height to object height.

subquestions.stem: Magnification is defined as:
subquestions.explanation: m = −d_i/d_o (also equals h_i/h_o).

items.stem: In infrared (IR) spectroscopy, absorption of IR radiation by a molecule primarily causes:
items.explanation: IR photon energies match molecular vibrational transitions, so absorption excites bond stretching and bending. Characteristic bands (e.g., a strong C=O stretch near 1700 cm⁻¹, a broad O–H stretch near 3300 cm⁻¹) reveal functional groups. Nuclear spin transitions are the domain of NMR.

source.name: OpenStax Organic Chemistry, Ch. 12: Structure Determination — Infrared Spectroscopy

subquestions.stem: IR spectroscopy probes which kind of molecular motion?
subquestions.explanation: IR excites vibrational modes of bonds.

subquestions.stem: A strong IR absorption near 1700 cm⁻¹ is characteristic of which group?
subquestions.explanation: The C=O stretch is a diagnostic strong band around 1700 cm⁻¹.

subquestions.stem: A broad IR band around 3300 cm⁻¹ typically indicates:
subquestions.explanation: Hydrogen-bonded O–H (alcohols/acids) gives a broad band near 3300 cm⁻¹.

items.stem: A compound appears colored because it absorbs visible light. Increasing the extent of conjugation in a molecule generally shifts its absorption toward:
items.explanation: Extended conjugation narrows the HOMO–LUMO energy gap, so lower-energy (longer-wavelength) photons are absorbed. Highly conjugated molecules such as β-carotene absorb in the visible range and appear colored. UV-Vis spectroscopy measures these electronic transitions.

source.name: OpenStax Organic Chemistry, Ch. 14: Conjugated Compounds and Ultraviolet Spectroscopy

subquestions.stem: UV-Vis spectroscopy involves transitions of:
subquestions.explanation: UV-Vis excites valence electrons between orbitals.

subquestions.stem: A smaller HOMO–LUMO gap corresponds to absorbing light of:
subquestions.explanation: ΔE = hf; a smaller gap means lower-frequency, longer-wavelength light.

subquestions.stem: Increasing conjugation length makes the electronic energy gap:
subquestions.explanation: More conjugation → smaller gap → red-shifted absorption.

items.stem: An atom has 17 protons, 18 neutrons, and 17 electrons. What are its mass number and net charge?
items.explanation: Mass number A = protons + neutrons = 17 + 18 = 35. Net charge = protons − electrons = 17 − 17 = 0 (neutral). This is a neutral chlorine-35 atom. The distractor 52 wrongly adds electrons into the mass number.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 2: Atoms, Molecules, and Ions

subquestions.stem: The mass number of an atom equals:
subquestions.explanation: Mass number counts protons plus neutrons (the nucleons).

subquestions.stem: An atom's net charge is determined by:
subquestions.explanation: Equal protons and electrons give a neutral atom.

subquestions.stem: Isotopes of the same element differ in their number of:
subquestions.explanation: Same protons (element identity), different neutrons (mass).

items.stem: What is the ground-state electron configuration of a neutral oxygen atom (Z = 8)?
items.explanation: Oxygen has 8 electrons: 1s² (2) + 2s² (2) + 2p⁴ (4) = 8, filled by the Aufbau principle. A 2p⁶ configuration (10 electrons) would be neon.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 6: Electronic Structure and Periodic Properties

subquestions.stem: Electrons fill orbitals from lowest to highest energy following the:
subquestions.explanation: Aufbau ('building up') orders orbital filling by energy.

subquestions.stem: A neutral oxygen atom has how many electrons?
subquestions.explanation: Neutral atoms have electrons equal to the atomic number, Z = 8.

subquestions.stem: The maximum number of electrons in a p subshell is:
subquestions.explanation: Three p orbitals × 2 electrons = 6.

items.stem: Which set of quantum numbers is NOT allowed for an electron?
items.explanation: For a given n, the angular-momentum quantum number l ranges from 0 to n−1. With n = 2, l can be 0 or 1 only, so n = 2, l = 2 is forbidden. The other sets are valid (for n = 3, l may be 0, 1, or 2, and m_l ranges −l..+l).

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 6: Development of Quantum Theory (Quantum Numbers)

subquestions.stem: The angular-momentum quantum number l can range from:
subquestions.explanation: l takes integer values 0 up to n−1.

subquestions.stem: For n = 2, the allowed values of l are:
subquestions.explanation: n = 2 allows l = 0 (s) and l = 1 (p) only.

subquestions.stem: The magnetic quantum number m_l ranges over:
subquestions.explanation: m_l runs in integer steps from −l to +l.

items.stem: An electron in a hydrogen atom drops from a higher to a lower energy level, emitting a photon. A transition that emits a higher-energy photon (versus a lower-energy one) produces light of:
items.explanation: Photon energy E = hf = hc/λ. A larger energy gap gives a higher frequency and therefore a shorter wavelength. Discrete atomic energy levels produce line (rather than continuous) spectra.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 6: The Bohr Model

subquestions.stem: A photon's energy is related to its frequency by:
subquestions.explanation: Energy is proportional to frequency: E = hf.

subquestions.stem: A higher-energy photon has a ______ wavelength.
subquestions.explanation: E = hc/λ, so higher energy means shorter wavelength.

subquestions.stem: Atoms emit discrete spectral lines because their energy levels are:
subquestions.explanation: Only specific energy gaps are allowed, giving specific photon energies.

items.stem: In the photoelectric effect, no electrons are ejected from a metal unless the light's frequency exceeds a threshold, no matter how intense the light. This demonstrates that light:
options: behaves purely as a continuous wave
items.explanation: A threshold frequency (below which no electrons are emitted regardless of intensity) shows light energy is quantized: E = hf per photon. Each photon must individually supply at least the work function to eject an electron; more intensity means more photons, not more energy per photon.

source.name: OpenStax College Physics 2e, Ch. 29: Introduction to Quantum Physics (Photoelectric Effect)

subquestions.stem: The energy of a single photon depends on the light's:
subquestions.explanation: Photon energy E = hf depends on frequency, not brightness.

subquestions.stem: Below the threshold frequency, increasing intensity will:
subquestions.explanation: If no single photon has enough energy, adding more of them still ejects none.

subquestions.stem: The minimum energy needed to remove an electron from the metal is the:
subquestions.explanation: The work function is the metal's electron-binding threshold.

items.stem: Moving left to right across a period of the periodic table, atomic radius generally:
items.explanation: Across a period, protons are added to the same principal shell, raising the effective nuclear charge and pulling electrons inward, so radius decreases. Down a group, additional shells are added, so radius increases.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 6: Periodic Variations in Element Properties

subquestions.stem: Atomic radius generally ______ down a group.
subquestions.explanation: Each new period adds an electron shell, enlarging the atom.

subquestions.stem: Across a period, the effective nuclear charge felt by valence electrons:
subquestions.explanation: More protons with the same shielding raises Z_eff.

subquestions.stem: A higher effective nuclear charge pulls the electron cloud:
subquestions.explanation: Stronger net pull contracts the atom.

items.stem: Which period-3 element has the highest first ionization energy: Na, Mg, Al, or Cl?
items.explanation: First ionization energy generally increases left → right across a period (higher Z_eff, smaller radius). Of these, chlorine is farthest right, so it holds its electrons most tightly and has the highest first ionization energy; sodium (far left) has the lowest.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 6: Periodic Variations (Ionization Energy)

subquestions.stem: Ionization energy is the energy required to:
subquestions.explanation: It is the energy to remove an electron from a gaseous atom.

subquestions.stem: First ionization energy generally ______ across a period (left to right).
subquestions.explanation: Rising Z_eff makes electrons harder to remove.

subquestions.stem: Down a group, first ionization energy generally:
subquestions.explanation: Valence electrons lie farther from the nucleus and are easier to remove.

items.stem: Which of these elements is the most electronegative?
items.explanation: Electronegativity increases up and to the right of the periodic table; fluorine is the most electronegative element (≈ 4.0 on the Pauling scale). The order here is F > O > C > Li.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 7: Chemical Bonding — Electronegativity

subquestions.stem: Electronegativity measures an atom's ability to:
subquestions.explanation: It is the pull an atom exerts on a shared electron pair.

subquestions.stem: Electronegativity generally increases toward the:
subquestions.explanation: It rises up and to the right (peaking at fluorine).

subquestions.stem: A large electronegativity difference between bonded atoms creates a bond that is:
subquestions.explanation: Unequal sharing produces bond polarity, and a large enough difference gives ionic character.

items.stem: A nucleus undergoes alpha decay. How do its atomic number (Z) and mass number (A) change?
items.explanation: An alpha particle is a helium-4 nucleus (2 protons, 2 neutrons). Emitting it decreases Z by 2 and A by 4. (For comparison, β⁻ decay leaves A unchanged and raises Z by 1 as a neutron becomes a proton plus an electron.)

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 21: Nuclear Chemistry (Radioactive Decay)

subquestions.stem: An alpha particle is identical to a:
subquestions.explanation: It contains 2 protons and 2 neutrons.

subquestions.stem: In beta-minus (β⁻) decay, a neutron converts into a proton plus:
subquestions.explanation: β⁻ decay emits an electron (and an antineutrino).

subquestions.stem: Beta-minus decay changes the atomic number by:
subquestions.explanation: Converting a neutron to a proton raises Z by 1 (A stays the same).

items.stem: A radioactive isotope has a half-life of 8 days. What fraction of the original sample remains after 24 days?
items.explanation: 24 days is 24/8 = 3 half-lives. After n half-lives the fraction remaining is (1/2)ⁿ = (1/2)³ = 1/8. The distractor 1/3 wrongly treats the number of half-lives as a simple fraction.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 21: Nuclear Chemistry (Half-Life)

subquestions.stem: After one half-life, the fraction of a sample remaining is:
subquestions.explanation: By definition, half remains after one half-life.

subquestions.stem: How many half-lives fit in 24 days if the half-life is 8 days?
subquestions.explanation: 24 / 8 = 3 half-lives.

subquestions.stem: Evaluate (1/2)³.

items.stem: How many moles are present in 36 g of water (molar mass 18 g/mol)?
items.explanation: Moles = mass ÷ molar mass = 36 g ÷ 18 g/mol = 2.0 mol. Distractors invert the ratio (0.5) or multiply (648).

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 3: Composition of Substances (The Mole)

subquestions.stem: The number of moles is calculated as:
subquestions.explanation: n = m / M.

subquestions.stem: Evaluate 36 g ÷ 18 g/mol.
How many molecules are in 2.0 mol of water? (N_A = 6.0×10²³ /mol)
subquestions.explanation: 2.0 mol × 6.0×10²³ = 1.2×10²⁴ molecules.

items.stem: For N₂ + 3 H₂ → 2 NH₃, starting with 1.0 mol N₂ and 1.0 mol H₂, which reactant is limiting?
items.explanation: The equation needs 3 mol H₂ per 1 mol N₂. With only 1.0 mol H₂, just 1/3 mol N₂ can react, so H₂ is consumed first and is the limiting reagent (excess N₂ remains).

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 4: Stoichiometry — Limiting Reactant

subquestions.stem: The limiting reagent is the reactant that:
subquestions.explanation: It runs out first and caps the amount of product.

subquestions.stem: How many moles of H₂ are needed to react completely with 1.0 mol N₂?
subquestions.explanation: The 1:3 mole ratio requires 3.0 mol H₂.

subquestions.stem: With 1.0 mol H₂ limiting, the maximum NH₃ produced is:
subquestions.explanation: 1.0 mol H₂ × (2 mol NH₃ / 3 mol H₂) = 0.67 mol NH₃.

items.stem: What is the pH of a 0.0010 M solution of HCl (a strong acid) at 25 °C?
items.explanation: HCl dissociates completely, so [H⁺] = 1.0×10⁻³ M and pH = −log(10⁻³) = 3. The value 11 is the pOH (14 − 3), not the pH.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 14: Acid-Base Equilibria (pH and pOH)

subquestions.stem: pH is defined as:
subquestions.explanation: pH = −log of the hydrogen-ion concentration.

subquestions.stem: Because HCl is a strong acid, [H⁺] equals:
subquestions.explanation: Complete dissociation makes [H⁺] equal to the acid concentration.

subquestions.stem: Evaluate −log(1×10⁻³).

items.stem: A solution at 25 °C has a pOH of 9. What is its pH?
items.explanation: At 25 °C, pH + pOH = 14 (from Kw = 1.0×10⁻¹⁴). So pH = 14 − 9 = 5. Because pH < 7, the solution is acidic.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 14: The Ionization of Water

subquestions.stem: At 25 °C, pH + pOH equals:
subquestions.explanation: The sum is 14 at standard temperature.

subquestions.stem: A solution with pH = 5 is:
subquestions.explanation: pH below 7 is acidic.

subquestions.stem: Kw at 25 °C equals:
subquestions.explanation: The ion-product of water is 1.0×10⁻¹⁴.

items.stem: Compared with a strong acid at the same concentration, a weak-acid solution has:
items.explanation: A weak acid only partially ionizes, so it produces fewer H⁺ ions than a strong acid at equal concentration. Lower [H⁺] means a higher (less acidic) pH, though it can still be below 7.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 14: Relative Strengths of Acids and Bases

subquestions.stem: A weak acid in water:
subquestions.explanation: Only a fraction of weak-acid molecules donate their proton.

subquestions.stem: Fewer H⁺ ions in solution give a ______ pH.
subquestions.explanation: Less [H⁺] → higher pH.

subquestions.stem: The acid-dissociation constant Ka of a weak acid is:
subquestions.explanation: A small Ka reflects limited ionization.

items.stem: What is the conjugate base of the ammonium ion, NH₄⁺?
items.explanation: A conjugate base is what remains after an acid donates one proton: NH₄⁺ − H⁺ → NH₃. Conjugate acid-base pairs differ by exactly one H⁺.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 14: Brønsted-Lowry Acids and Bases

subquestions.stem: A conjugate base forms when an acid:
subquestions.explanation: Donating H⁺ converts an acid into its conjugate base.

subquestions.stem: Members of a conjugate acid-base pair differ by:
subquestions.explanation: They differ by a single H⁺.

subquestions.stem: The conjugate acid of water (H₂O) is:
subquestions.explanation: Adding a proton to water gives hydronium, H₃O⁺.

items.stem: A buffer contains equal concentrations of a weak acid (pKa = 4.7) and its conjugate base. What is the pH of the buffer?
items.explanation: Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]). With [A⁻] = [HA], log(1) = 0, so pH = pKa = 4.7. A buffer resists pH change and is most effective near its pKa.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 14: Buffers

subquestions.stem: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation is:
subquestions.explanation: It expresses pH in terms of pKa and the base:acid ratio.

subquestions.stem: When [A⁻] = [HA], the term log([A⁻]/[HA]) equals:
subquestions.explanation: log(1) = 0, so pH = pKa.

subquestions.stem: A buffer resists pH change most effectively when the pH is:
subquestions.explanation: Buffering capacity is greatest within about ±1 pH unit of the pKa.

items.stem: In the titration of a strong acid with a strong base, the pH at the equivalence point is approximately:
items.explanation: At the equivalence point, moles of acid = moles of base. For a strong acid/strong base pair the resulting salt (e.g., NaCl) does not hydrolyze, so the solution is neutral: pH ≈ 7 at 25 °C. A weak-acid/strong-base titration would give an equivalence-point pH above 7.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 14: Acid-Base Titrations

subquestions.stem: The equivalence point of a titration is where:
options: moles of acid stoichiometrically equal moles of base
subquestions.explanation: It is the point of stoichiometric neutralization.

subquestions.stem: The salt of a strong acid and strong base (e.g., NaCl) makes a solution that is:
subquestions.explanation: Neither ion hydrolyzes, so pH ≈ 7.

subquestions.stem: A weak-acid/strong-base titration has an equivalence-point pH that is:
subquestions.explanation: The conjugate base left behind is weakly basic, raising pH above 7.

items.stem: For AgCl dissolving in pure water with molar solubility s, AgCl ⇌ Ag⁺ + Cl⁻. Which expression equals Ksp?
items.explanation: Each formula unit gives one Ag⁺ and one Cl⁻, so [Ag⁺] = [Cl⁻] = s and Ksp = (s)(s) = s². (For a 1:2 salt like CaF₂ → Ca²⁺ + 2 F⁻, Ksp = 4s³.)

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 15: Solubility Product (Ksp)

subquestions.stem: Ksp is the equilibrium constant for:
subquestions.explanation: It describes the dissolution equilibrium of a sparingly soluble ionic solid.

subquestions.stem: For AgCl → Ag⁺ + Cl⁻ with solubility s, [Ag⁺] equals:
subquestions.explanation: The 1:1 stoichiometry gives [Ag⁺] = s.

subquestions.stem: Adding NaCl (supplying the common ion Cl⁻) to saturated AgCl will:
subquestions.explanation: The common-ion effect shifts the equilibrium toward solid, lowering solubility.

items.stem: Water has an unusually high boiling point for such a small molecule. This is primarily due to:
options: the covalent bonds within each molecule
items.explanation: Water molecules form extensive intermolecular hydrogen bonds (O–H···O). Breaking these on boiling requires substantial energy, giving water its anomalously high boiling point, high specific heat, and high heat of vaporization relative to its molar mass.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 10: Liquids and Solids — Hydrogen Bonding

subquestions.stem: Hydrogen bonding requires H to be bonded to which kind of atom?
options: a highly electronegative atom (O, N, or F)
subquestions.explanation: Strong H-bonds form when H is bound to N, O, or F.

subquestions.stem: Water's high specific heat means it:
subquestions.explanation: Much energy goes into breaking H-bonds rather than raising temperature.

subquestions.stem: Ice floats on liquid water because, on freezing, water becomes:
subquestions.explanation: The open hydrogen-bonded lattice of ice is less dense than liquid water.

items.stem: In pure water at 25 °C, the hydronium-ion concentration [H₃O⁺] is:
items.explanation: Water autoionizes: 2 H₂O ⇌ H₃O⁺ + OH⁻, Kw = [H₃O⁺][OH⁻] = 1.0×10⁻¹⁴. In pure water [H₃O⁺] = [OH⁻] = √(10⁻¹⁴) = 1.0×10⁻⁷ M, giving a neutral pH of 7.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 14: The Ionization of Water (Kw)

subquestions.stem: Autoionization of water produces equal amounts of:
subquestions.explanation: One water acts as an acid, another as a base.

subquestions.stem: In pure water, [H₃O⁺] compared with [OH⁻] is:
subquestions.explanation: They are equal, which defines neutrality.

subquestions.stem: Evaluate √(1×10⁻¹⁴).

items.stem: According to the Lewis definition, an acid is a substance that:
items.explanation: A Lewis acid is an electron-pair acceptor; a Lewis base is an electron-pair donor. (The Brønsted-Lowry model instead defines an acid as a proton donor.) BF₃ is a classic Lewis acid, NH₃ a Lewis base.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 15: Lewis Acids and Bases

subquestions.stem: A Lewis base is an electron-pair:
subquestions.explanation: Lewis bases donate a lone pair to form a bond.

subquestions.stem: The Brønsted-Lowry definition of an acid is a:
subquestions.explanation: Brønsted acids donate H⁺.

subquestions.stem: BF₃, which has an incomplete octet on boron, acts as a:
subquestions.explanation: The electron-deficient boron accepts an electron pair.

items.stem: How many grams of NaOH (molar mass 40 g/mol) are needed to prepare 2.0 L of a 0.50 M solution?
items.explanation: Moles = M × V = (0.50 mol/L)(2.0 L) = 1.0 mol; mass = moles × molar mass = (1.0)(40) = 40 g. Distractors drop the volume or molar-mass factor.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 3: Composition of Substances and Solutions (Molarity)

subquestions.stem: Molarity is defined as:
options: moles of solute per liter of solution
subquestions.explanation: Molarity = mol solute / L solution.

subquestions.stem: Moles of NaOH needed = M × V = 0.50 × 2.0 = ?
Convert 1.0 mol NaOH to grams (40 g/mol).
subquestions.explanation: 1.0 mol × 40 g/mol = 40 g.

items.stem: A chemical bond formed by the essentially complete transfer of electrons from a metal to a nonmetal is best classified as:
items.explanation: A large electronegativity difference (typically metal + nonmetal) leads to electron transfer and formation of oppositely charged ions held together electrostatically — an ionic bond. Covalent bonds share electrons; metallic bonding features delocalized electrons among metal cations.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 7: Ionic and Covalent Bonding

subquestions.stem: Covalent bonds involve electrons that are:
subquestions.explanation: Covalent bonding is electron sharing.

subquestions.stem: Ionic bonds typically form between:
subquestions.explanation: Large electronegativity differences favor ionic bonding.

subquestions.stem: The attraction holding an ionic compound together is between:
subquestions.explanation: Cations and anions attract electrostatically.

items.stem: How many valence electrons must be shown in the Lewis structure of carbon dioxide, CO₂?
items.explanation: Total valence electrons = 4 (C) + 2×6 (O) = 16. These form two C=O double bonds so every atom reaches an octet. The distractor 22 mistakenly includes core electrons.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 7: Lewis Symbols and Structures

subquestions.stem: Carbon contributes how many valence electrons?
subquestions.explanation: Carbon is in group 14, so 4 valence electrons.

subquestions.stem: Each oxygen contributes how many valence electrons?
subquestions.explanation: Oxygen is in group 16, so 6 valence electrons.

subquestions.stem: Evaluate 4 + 2×6.
subquestions.explanation: 4 + 12 = 16 valence electrons total.

items.stem: According to VSEPR theory, what is the molecular geometry of methane, CH₄?
items.explanation: Carbon in CH₄ has 4 bonding pairs and no lone pairs. To minimize repulsion, the four domains point to the corners of a tetrahedron (bond angles ≈ 109.5°), giving a tetrahedral molecular geometry.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 7: Molecular Structure and Polarity (VSEPR)

subquestions.stem: VSEPR theory positions electron domains so as to:
subquestions.explanation: Domains spread out to minimize electron-pair repulsion.

subquestions.stem: How many bonding electron domains surround carbon in CH₄?
subquestions.explanation: Four C–H bonds means four bonding domains.

subquestions.stem: The H–C–H bond angle in tetrahedral methane is about:
subquestions.explanation: The ideal tetrahedral angle is 109.5°.

items.stem: Oxygen in a water molecule (H₂O) has two bonding pairs and two lone pairs. What is water's molecular geometry?
items.explanation: Four electron domains (2 bonding + 2 lone pairs) give a tetrahedral electron geometry, but molecular geometry names only atom positions, so water is bent (≈ 104.5°). Lone-pair repulsion pushes the bond angle below the ideal 109.5°.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 7: Molecular Structure and Polarity

subquestions.stem: How many total electron domains surround the oxygen in water?
subquestions.explanation: Two bonds plus two lone pairs = four domains.

subquestions.stem: When naming molecular shape, lone pairs are:
options: counted as if they were atoms
not counted as atoms but still repel bonds
subquestions.explanation: Molecular geometry describes atom positions only, though lone pairs still influence angles.

subquestions.stem: Lone-pair/bonding-pair repulsion makes the H–O–H angle:
subquestions.explanation: Lone pairs repel more strongly, compressing the angle to ~104.5°.

items.stem: What is the hybridization of each carbon atom in ethene (H₂C=CH₂)?
items.explanation: Each ethene carbon forms 3 sigma bonds (two C–H, one C–C) and participates in one pi bond — 3 electron domains → sp² hybridization (trigonal planar, ~120°). The unhybridized p orbital forms the pi bond.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 8: Advanced Theories of Covalent Bonding (Hybridization)

subquestions.stem: The number of hybrid orbitals equals the number of:
subquestions.explanation: Count sigma bonds plus lone pairs to get the hybridization.

subquestions.stem: A carbon involved in one double bond has how many sigma/lone-pair domains?
subquestions.explanation: Three domains (the pi bond does not count separately) → sp².

subquestions.stem: An sp²-hybridized carbon has what local geometry?
subquestions.explanation: Three domains arrange trigonal planar at ~120°.

items.stem: Carbon dioxide (O=C=O) has two polar C=O bonds, yet the molecule as a whole is nonpolar. Why?
options: its linear shape makes the two bond dipoles cancel
items.explanation: CO₂ is linear, so the two equal C=O bond dipoles point in opposite directions and cancel, giving zero net dipole. Overall polarity depends on both bond polarity and molecular geometry — bent H₂O is polar because its dipoles do not cancel.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 7: Molecular Structure and Polarity (Dipole Moments)

subquestions.stem: A covalent bond is polar when the two atoms differ in:
subquestions.explanation: An electronegativity difference creates a bond dipole.

subquestions.stem: Whether a molecule is polar overall depends on bond polarity and:
subquestions.explanation: Geometry decides whether bond dipoles cancel.

subquestions.stem: Why is bent H₂O polar while linear CO₂ is not?
options: water's bent shape prevents dipole cancellation
subquestions.explanation: In water the dipoles add to a net moment; in linear CO₂ they cancel.

items.stem: A carbon-carbon triple bond (C≡C) consists of:
options: two sigma bonds and one pi bond
one sigma bond and two pi bonds
items.explanation: A triple bond is one sigma bond (head-on orbital overlap) plus two pi bonds (side-on p-orbital overlap). Single bond = 1 sigma; double = 1 sigma + 1 pi; triple = 1 sigma + 2 pi.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 8: Sigma and Pi Bonds

subquestions.stem: The first bond formed between two atoms is always a:
subquestions.explanation: Sigma bonds form by direct, head-on overlap and always come first.

subquestions.stem: A double bond contains:
options: one sigma and one pi bond
subquestions.explanation: A double bond is 1 sigma + 1 pi.

subquestions.stem: Pi bonds form by ______ overlap of p orbitals.
subquestions.explanation: Parallel p orbitals overlap sideways to make a pi bond.

items.stem: Which of the following intermolecular forces is generally the strongest?
items.explanation: Of the common intermolecular forces, hydrogen bonding is the strongest — a specially strong dipole-dipole interaction that occurs when H is bonded to N, O, or F. General order: hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole > London dispersion. (Ion-dipole is stronger still but requires an ion.)

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 10: Intermolecular Forces

subquestions.stem: London dispersion forces arise from:
subquestions.explanation: Fluctuating electron distributions create momentary dipoles.

subquestions.stem: Hydrogen bonding is a particularly strong form of:
subquestions.explanation: It is an unusually strong dipole-dipole attraction.

subquestions.stem: Larger molecules tend to have stronger dispersion forces because they have:
subquestions.explanation: More electrons → larger, more polarizable clouds → stronger dispersion.

items.stem: Two liquids have similar molar masses, but liquid X can hydrogen-bond while liquid Y cannot. Which has the higher boiling point?
items.explanation: Boiling requires overcoming intermolecular forces. Hydrogen bonding (in X) is stronger than the dipole/dispersion forces available to Y, so X requires more energy to vaporize and boils higher (e.g., ethanol vs. dimethyl ether of similar mass).

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 10: Properties of Liquids (Boiling Point)

subquestions.stem: Boiling a liquid requires overcoming its:
subquestions.explanation: Vaporization separates molecules by breaking intermolecular attractions.

subquestions.stem: Stronger intermolecular forces produce a ______ boiling point.
subquestions.explanation: More energy is needed to separate the molecules.

subquestions.stem: Which molecule can hydrogen-bond with others of its own kind?
subquestions.explanation: The O–H group of methanol enables hydrogen bonding.

items.stem: In the Lewis structure of the hydronium ion (H₃O⁺), what is the formal charge on the oxygen atom?
items.explanation: Formal charge = valence electrons − nonbonding electrons − ½(bonding electrons). Oxygen in H₃O⁺ has 6 valence, 2 nonbonding (one lone pair), and 6 bonding electrons: 6 − 2 − 3 = +1, consistent with the ion's overall +1 charge.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 7: Formal Charges and Resonance

subquestions.stem: Formal charge is calculated as:
subquestions.explanation: FC = valence − lone-pair electrons − half the shared electrons.

subquestions.stem: In H₃O⁺, oxygen makes 3 bonds and carries how many lone pairs?
subquestions.explanation: Three bonds use six electrons, leaving one lone pair.

subquestions.stem: The sum of all formal charges in a polyatomic ion equals:
subquestions.explanation: Formal charges sum to the total charge of the species.

items.stem: The nitrate ion (NO₃⁻) is drawn with three resonance structures. The actual bonding in nitrate is best described as:
options: one fixed double bond and two single bonds
a resonance hybrid with three equivalent N–O bonds
items.explanation: The three resonance structures average into one resonance hybrid: all three N–O bonds are equivalent, of identical intermediate length (bond order ≈ 1.33). Delocalizing the electrons stabilizes the ion. No single localized double bond exists.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e, Ch. 7: Formal Charges and Resonance

subquestions.stem: Resonance structures differ only in the placement of:
subquestions.explanation: Only electron positions change; the atomic skeleton is fixed.

subquestions.stem: The true structure (the resonance hybrid) is:
options: a weighted average of all resonance forms
subquestions.explanation: The real ion is a single averaged structure.

subquestions.stem: In nitrate, the three N–O bonds are:
